Paper 2 (MGDebugger) has higher estimated scientific impact due to a more novel and generally applicable conceptual contribution: hierarchical, multi-granularity debugging with bottom-up repair, which can influence LLM code generation, automated program repair, and software engineering workflows broadly. Its reported gains are large and consistent across models/datasets, suggesting meaningful methodological advancement beyond incremental tuning. Paper 1 (EvAFuzz) targets an important niche (DL library fuzzing) but appears more incremental (small crash-count improvement, confounded by different LLM backbones, limited statistical control), which may limit perceived novelty and broader uptake. While MGDebugger has unresolved concerns (simulated execution reliability, cost, scalability to real repos), it addresses a timely, widely relevant “last-mile correctness” bottleneck with a framework likely to inspire follow-on work across fields.

LILO (Paper 2) demonstrates higher scientific impact potential because it addresses a fundamental problem in neurosymbolic AI: how to make learned symbolic abstractions usable by neural models. While EvAFuzz (Paper 1) offers a solid engineering improvement in fuzzing, it is an incremental combination of known techniques (LLMs + Evolutionary Algorithms). LILO, conversely, offers a novel insight—that 'anonymous' symbolic abstractions act as obfuscation to LLMs, and that automated documentation (AutoDoc) is a functional requirement for neurosymbolic performance, not just a human aid. This finding has broader implications for the design of AI agents and tool-using systems beyond just program synthesis. LILO also dramatically reduces the computational cost of library learning (replacing CPU-months with hours), making a previously niche technique accessible for wider research.

CodeDPO introduces a higher degree of algorithmic novelty with its PageRank-inspired mutual validation system, which elegantly solves the 'chicken-and-egg' problem of evaluating generated code without pre-existing test suites or external oracle models like GPT-4. While PLUM offers a solid engineering integration of on-policy learning, it relies heavily on external resources (GPT-4) for test generation. CodeDPO's self-contained nature, combined with its dual focus on correctness and efficiency, offers a more methodologically rigorous and scalable path forward for self-improving models, a critical area of research. The ability to generate high-quality preference data without a stronger teacher model gives CodeDPO higher potential impact for the democratization of model alignment.

L2MAC demonstrates higher scientific impact due to its stronger empirical results with large margins over competitive baselines (59-92% vs 10-53%), more practical real-world applicability in code generation, and a principled architectural framework (von Neumann analogy) that provides a reusable template for LLM-based multi-agent systems. Its comprehensive evaluation (10 seeds, 95% CIs, human validation, extensive ablations) demonstrates greater methodological rigor than STOP's small-scale proof-of-concept (5 runs on toy algorithmic tasks). While STOP is conceptually creative in exploring recursive self-improvement of scaffolding, its practical impact is limited by high cost, model dependence (only works with GPT-4), weak baselines, and small-scale tasks. L2MAC addresses a more pressing practical bottleneck (generating large, consistent codebases) and its design principles (P1-P3) offer diagnostic value for the broader multi-agent systems community.

InsightBench introduces a novel evaluation paradigm for data analytics agents, shifting from single-query benchmarks to end-to-end analytics assessment—a fundamentally new way of thinking about agent evaluation. Published at ICLR 2025, it addresses a widely recognized gap as LLM-based analytics tools proliferate. Its framework (insight taxonomy, two-way evaluation, open-source evaluator validation) is broadly transferable across domains. EvAFuzz, while a solid engineering contribution combining evolutionary algorithms with LLM fuzzing, represents a more incremental advance over existing work (TitanFuzz, FuzzGPT) with modest quantitative improvements (9 vs 7 crashes, using a different LLM backbone) and methodological concerns around controlled comparisons. InsightBench's paradigm-defining nature, broader applicability across the rapidly growing agent evaluation space, and stronger reproducibility (open-source data, code, metrics) give it higher potential for community adoption and downstream influence.

Paper 2 (RLSF) has higher potential scientific impact because it introduces a broadly reusable training paradigm—RL fine-tuning driven by symbolic verifiers that yield dense, token-level feedback—addressing core RLHF pain points (reward model brittleness, sparsity, and human data cost). Its applicability spans multiple high-value structured domains (software, formal math, chemistry, hardware/protocol verification), and it can leverage existing industrial-strength toolchains (compilers, RDKit, SymPy), making real-world uptake plausible. While ChartMimic (Paper 1) is a strong, timely benchmark with rigorous evaluation infrastructure, its impact is more confined to multimodal chart/code tasks and benchmarking. RLSF is more likely to alter how domain-specific LLMs are trained, with cross-field methodological influence beyond any single dataset.

ML-Bench addresses a broader and more timely need in the rapidly growing LLM-for-code and autonomous agent ecosystem, providing infrastructure (9,641 examples, 18 repositories, Docker environments, multiple agent framework integrations) that can serve as a standard evaluation tool for a large community of researchers. Benchmarks that define evaluation standards tend to have outsized citation impact. Its dual-setup design (ML-Bench-L and ML-Bench-A) and compatibility with multiple agent frameworks position it for wide adoption. In contrast, EASE, while conceptually elegant, targets a narrower subfield (3D visual grounding), achieves only moderate absolute performance (50.7% on Nr3D), and its practical impact is constrained by geometric-only features and a limited relation vocabulary. ML-Bench's community utility as shared infrastructure gives it higher potential for broad, sustained scientific impact.

Paper 1 has higher likely scientific impact due to a more novel algorithmic shift (bringing value-based/off-policy RL to LLM program synthesis, plus Q-initialization from LM logits and a conservative Bellman operator) that can transfer beyond code to other verifiable generation tasks. It is methodologically more rigorous (explicit operators, contraction guarantees, theoretical links between Q and recovered rewards) and comparatively reproducible (does not hinge on proprietary 62B models). Its “dual-use” value function enabling test-free candidate ranking is a broadly useful mechanism with clear downstream application potential. Paper 2 targets an important practical problem, but its impact is constrained by reliance on anonymized proprietary models, narrower domain scope (data science-centric), and weaker/less fair baselines, making it harder for the community to validate and build on.

While Paper 1 offers a valuable critique of an existing benchmark (SWE-bench), Paper 2 (BigCodeBench) introduces a comprehensive, rigorous, and much-needed new standard for evaluating LLMs on practical, library-dependent coding tasks. BigCodeBench fills a critical gap between simple algorithmic puzzles (HumanEval) and complex repository-level agents (SWE-bench). Its methodology is robust (high test coverage, extensive human curation), and its findings regarding 'model laziness' and instruction-following gaps provide immediate actionable insights for model developers. Paper 1's impact is limited by the small size of its resulting dataset and its inability to solve the 'weak test' problem it identifies, whereas BigCodeBench provides a turnkey, scalable evaluation infrastructure likely to be widely adopted as a standard metric.

Spider 2.0 represents a major benchmark release that will likely define the field of Text-to-SQL for years to come. By showing that SOTA models drop from ~90% on previous benchmarks to ~20% on this new one, it establishes a massive 'headroom' for future research and effectively deprecates older datasets. The shift towards enterprise-scale, agentic, and multi-dialect SQL workflows addresses a critical saturation point in current literature. While Paper 1 provides a valuable negative result and diagnostic insight regarding self-debugging, it is an incremental empirical study with modest improvements. Spider 2.0 is a foundational infrastructure contribution that will enable and measure progress for the entire community.

Arctic-SnowCoder addresses a more fundamental and broadly applicable question—what constitutes high-quality pretraining data—that affects the entire LLM community, not just DL library testing. Its comprehensive ablation studies across quality annotators, learning rate schedules, data repetition, and training phases provide actionable insights transferable to other domains beyond code. The finding that a 1.3B model on 555B tokens can match models trained on trillions of tokens has significant practical implications for compute-constrained organizations. While both papers are empirical engineering contributions, Arctic-SnowCoder's scope of impact is broader: data curation methodology affects every LLM practitioner, whereas EvAFuzz targets a niche (important but narrow) application of DL library fuzzing with modest incremental improvements (9 vs 7 crashes using different LLM backbones). Arctic-SnowCoder's systematic methodology also provides a more reusable template for future research.

Paper 2 has higher potential scientific impact because it introduces a genuinely new algorithmic direction—value-based deep RL for program synthesis—backed by principled theory (contraction guarantees, reward–Q bijection) and a transferable initialization method that bridges pretrained LM logits to Q-functions. Its ideas plausibly generalize beyond code to other verifiable sequence-generation domains, broadening cross-field influence. The “free” reward model for ranking without unit tests also targets a key real-world bottleneck. Paper 1 is timely and practically useful as an evaluation platform, but it is primarily an engineering/benchmark consolidation with narrower scope (Python-only, constrained attack environment) and impact that depends heavily on adoption and maintenance. Overall, Paper 2 is more likely to catalyze follow-on research and methodological reuse across communities.

BigCodeBench addresses a critical saturation point in current code generation benchmarks (HumanEval/MBPP) by introducing a rigorous, diverse, and practically relevant evaluation framework involving complex tool use and function calls. Its methodological rigor (high test coverage, human curation) and the discovery of novel phenomena like 'model laziness' give it high potential to become a standard community benchmark. In contrast, Effi-Code, while addressing the important niche of code efficiency, presents primarily a dataset engineering contribution with confounded results (it is unclear if gains are from efficiency training or general instruction tuning) and relies on aggressive filtering that raises scalability concerns. Experts are more likely to adopt BigCodeBench as a fundamental evaluation tool than Effi-Code's specific fine-tuning recipe.

Paper 2 (BigCodeBench) has higher likely scientific impact because it delivers a broadly useful, rigorous evaluation artifact that can become community infrastructure: 1,140 execution-verified tasks spanning 139 libraries/7 domains with high-coverage tests, strong contamination checks, and extensive human curation. Its findings (notably the quantified “instruction gap” and “model laziness”) are actionable for many LLM and agent researchers, affecting model training, prompting, and evaluation beyond any single application domain. In contrast, Paper 1 (Instruct2Act) is a timely robotics integration system but is constrained to structured tabletop pick-and-place, depends heavily on prompts/heuristics and large external models, and has some fairness/real-world evaluation limitations, reducing breadth and durability of impact. Overall, BigCodeBench is more general, methodologically durable, and likely to be adopted widely.

Paper 2 (CodeFavor) demonstrates substantially higher potential scientific impact for several reasons: (1) It addresses a timely and practically important problem—code preference learning for LLM alignment—with clear real-world applications in code review, RLHF pipelines, and automated evaluation. (2) It introduces both a reusable benchmark (CodePrefBench) with verifiable ground-truth labels and a practical training framework, both likely to see community adoption. (3) Its methodology is rigorous, with comprehensive ablations, external baselines (GPT-4, Claude, etc.), and a well-designed human study yielding surprising findings about human limitations. (4) Paper 1 (COOL) suffers from critical weaknesses: no external baselines, narrow evaluation domains, improvements that largely stem from encoding expert knowledge, and positioning that feels dated relative to LLM-based approaches. Paper 2's findings about human preference limitations and cost-effective model-based alternatives have broad implications for the rapidly growing code LLM ecosystem.

LILO (Paper 1) demonstrates significantly higher methodological rigor and clear novelty in the neurosymbolic domain. Its integration of symbolic compression (Stitch) with LLM-based documentation (AutoDoc) addresses a specific, demonstrated failure mode in AI-generated code libraries (opacity reducing performance). The evaluation is robust, comparing against strong baselines like DreamCoder across multiple domains. In contrast, LARG2 (Paper 2) suffers from major methodological weaknesses, including undefined metrics, lack of baselines, weak statistical reporting, and poor writing quality. While the topic of LARG2 is timely, it has been superseded by more rigorous concurrent works (e.g., Eureka), whereas LILO offers a unique architectural contribution to the library learning problem.

Spider 2.0 is a landmark benchmark contribution that fundamentally resets the evaluation landscape for text-to-SQL research. By exposing a massive performance gap (21.3% vs 91.2% on Spider 1.0), it provides years of research headroom and redirects the community toward unsolved enterprise-level challenges. Its real-world grounding (cloud databases, multiple SQL dialects, enterprise-scale schemas) ensures direct relevance to industry applications. The benchmark's influence extends beyond text-to-SQL into code agent evaluation more broadly. In contrast, RLCF presents a solid but incremental methodological contribution limited by small model scales (≤1.5B parameters), Java-only evaluation, modest improvements without statistical significance testing, and a rapidly evolving competitive landscape where larger-scale approaches have overtaken its contributions. Spider 2.0's potential to catalyze a new research direction gives it substantially higher expected impact.

Paper 1 is likely to have higher scientific impact because it couples a large, well-defined dataset of human performance-improving edits with a major methodological advance: deterministic, reproducible performance evaluation via gem5 to eliminate benchmarking noise—an issue that undermines much prior optimization work. It also proposes and systematically validates multiple adaptation strategies (prompting + finetuning + self-play), demonstrating substantial speedups and offering a general framework for LLM-driven semantic code optimization with clear systems and software-engineering applications. Paper 2 is timely and practically relevant as a repository-level benchmark, but its impact is more narrowly centered on evaluation; it faces concerns about task depth, potential training-data contamination, and limited agent-evaluation sample size, which may reduce community trust and downstream scientific leverage.

Paper 1 addresses the highly relevant and active field of LLM-based code generation using a method (iterative feedback and execution-based debugging) that directly aligns with current research trends and practical developer workflows. While it has methodological flaws (contradictory appendix examples, missing baselines), its core premise—leveraging runtime variable state to improve LLM self-correction—is a tangible contribution to the 'Agentic AI' paradigm. Paper 2, conversely, proposes a complex DSL-based synthesis framework requiring significant manual engineering. It suffers critically from a lack of external baselines (comparing only against itself) and evaluates on trivial, narrow tasks (family relations, quadratic equations) that do not demonstrate real-world utility in the modern synthesis landscape. Paper 1's relevance to the dominant LLM paradigm gives it significantly higher potential for citation and adoption.

ENAMEL introduces a principled statistical framework (eff@k with Rao-Blackwellization) that fills a genuine and widely-recognized gap in LLM code evaluation—efficiency measurement. Its formal guarantees, proper handling of right-censored data, and revelations about existing benchmark inadequacies (wrong canonical solutions, misleading speedup metrics) provide both methodological and empirical contributions with broad influence. The finding that top LLMs achieve high correctness but low efficiency scores quantifies a fundamental capability gap that will motivate efficiency-aware training. FLoRA, while solving a real deployment problem with an elegant algebraic trick, is more incremental—limited to low-rank regimes, incompatible with existing LoRA adapters, and narrower in scope. ENAMEL's framework is extensible across languages and domains, and its metrics could become standard complements to pass@k, giving it broader and more lasting impact across the code generation research community.

Paper 1 has higher likely scientific impact because it introduces a new, well-motivated evaluation dimension (self-consistency across NL↔code) that directly challenges accuracy-centric assessment and can reshape how Code LLMs are benchmarked and debugged. IdentityChain is broadly adoptable (works atop standard benchmarks), exposes distinct and actionable failure modes, and contributes an execution-based metric (TOM) with validation against human judgments—supporting methodological rigor and reproducibility (open-source). Its conceptual framing (round-trip/identity consistency) generalizes beyond code to other multimodal or dual-task settings, increasing breadth of impact. Paper 2 is timely and useful engineering for multi-agent orchestration, but its gains are more contingent on rapidly changing model APIs, rely heavily on proprietary systems, and its “dynamic” novelty is comparatively incremental, making longer-term scientific influence less certain.

Spider 2.0 represents a significantly larger leap in benchmark complexity and potential impact. While GitChameleon addresses a valid and specific problem (versioning), its small scale (116 samples) limits its utility as a standard benchmark. Spider 2.0, conversely, resets a major subfield (Text-to-SQL) by demonstrating that current 'solved' status on benchmarks like Spider 1.0 is illusory when applied to enterprise contexts. Its inclusion of diverse SQL dialects, massive real-world databases, and agentic workflows creates a long-term challenge for the community, similar to how the original Spider benchmark drove progress for years. The gap between current capability (21.3%) and the ceiling is massive, motivating extensive future research.

Paper 1 has higher potential scientific impact because it contributes both a broadly applicable agent system and a new domain-specific model (HTML-T5) with architectural inductive bias (local–global attention) and pretraining objectives tailored to structured HTML—advances that can generalize to other structured-document domains. Its demonstrated gains span real-world web automation plus multiple established benchmarks (MiniWoB++, Mind2Web, HTML understanding tasks), suggesting wider cross-field influence (agents, information extraction, program synthesis, HCI). The work is also timely for LLM agents moving beyond toy environments. Paper 2 is methodologically solid and practically useful for test-driven code generation, but is more of a workflow/prompting contribution tied to visible tests and single-function programming tasks, with narrower generalization and less fundamental modeling innovation.

SecCodePLT addresses a more pressing and broadly relevant problem—security evaluation of code generation AI—at a time when these tools are being rapidly deployed in production. Its unified platform combining dynamic evaluation for both insecure coding and cyberattack helpfulness fills a clear gap in the evaluation ecosystem, with direct implications for AI safety policy, model deployment decisions, and developer tool security. The practical findings (e.g., Cursor's security failures, differential refusal rates across models, effect of security reminders) have immediate actionable value for industry and policymakers. While EASE presents a conceptually clean neuro-symbolic framework, its modest absolute performance (50.7% on Nr3D), narrow scope (bounding-box geometry only, one benchmark), and incremental nature limit its broader impact. SecCodePLT's timeliness, cross-cutting relevance to AI safety and software engineering, and potential to influence evaluation norms give it higher impact potential.

Arctic-SnowCoder addresses a more fundamental and broadly applicable question—what constitutes high-quality pretraining data—with comprehensive ablation studies that provide actionable insights for the entire LLM pretraining community, not just code generation. Its findings about progressive data refinement and distributional alignment generalize beyond code to other domains. The paper demonstrates remarkable data efficiency (555B tokens matching models trained on 3T+ tokens), which has significant practical implications given growing concerns about the 'data wall.' In contrast, LPW/SLPW is a well-executed but incremental engineering contribution—a prompt engineering workflow specific to code generation with visible test cases. While LPW shows strong empirical results, its impact is narrower (single-function code generation), heavily dependent on prompt design, and its core insight (plan before coding) is less transferable. Arctic-SnowCoder's systematic methodology for data curation is more likely to influence future pretraining research broadly.

Paper 1 has higher expected scientific impact due to a concrete, novel, and timely contribution: an iterative VLM-based verification/refinement loop for CAD code generation plus a new benchmark (CADPrompt). It targets a high-value real-world domain (parametric CAD for design/manufacturing), is model-agnostic, and is evaluated with multiple baselines, metrics, and ablations, indicating reasonable methodological rigor despite modest gains and a small dataset. Paper 2, while claiming broad theory and multimodal novelty, appears methodologically non-reproducible and internally inconsistent: key definitions/theorems are missing, the “image-guided” mechanism is not specified, and experiments are narrow with insufficient implementation detail. Experts would therefore view Paper 1 as a credible, usable step forward with broader transfer potential, whereas Paper 2’s claims are not substantiated.

Paper 1 (CodeUpdateArena) addresses a more fundamental and pervasive problem in the field of Large Language Models: the 'staleness' of knowledge, specifically within the high-impact domain of code generation. By establishing the first benchmark for knowledge editing on API updates, it opens a new sub-field connecting two major research communities (Knowledge Editing and Code Intelligence). The finding that standard fine-tuning on documentation fails while usage examples succeed provides critical insight for maintaining the utility of coding assistants like Copilot. While Paper 2 (CADCodeVerify) offers a solid application in a niche domain (CAD) with a novel verification loop, its impact is narrower, and its improvements are incremental (7.3%). Paper 1's contribution to the foundational maintenance and reliability of code LLMs suggests a broader and more lasting scientific influence.

GitChameleon addresses a novel, practically important, and underexplored problem—version-aware code generation—by introducing an execution-based benchmark that fills a clear gap in the evaluation landscape. Its finding that even GPT-4 achieves only ~40% pass@10 provides a compelling, quantified motivation for future research in RAG, continual learning, and version-aware training for code LLMs. The benchmark is timely given HumanEval saturation and the rapid adoption of AI coding assistants in production environments with pinned dependencies. While small in scale (116 problems), its conceptual novelty and potential to catalyze a new research direction give it broader impact. Paper 1 (RLCF), while methodologically sound, represents a more incremental contribution to the already crowded RL-for-code space, is limited to small/dated model sizes, and its core claims about language-agnosticism remain unvalidated. The rapid evolution of RLHF methods has also partially overtaken its contributions.

Paper 2 (L2MAC) has higher likely scientific impact because it proposes a novel, general architectural framework for overcoming context limits in long-form generation via a stored-program, multi-agent “computer” with explicit instruction registry, modifiable file-store memory, and a control unit enabling precise read/write plus verification. This is broadly applicable beyond code (any long, structured artifact), timely for agentic LLM systems, and offers a clear conceptual lens (P1–P3) that can guide future designs. Its evaluation appears comparatively rigorous and diagnostics-driven (new benchmark, multiple baselines with tool parity, confidence intervals, mechanism analyses, and human validation of GPT-based metrics). Paper 1 is a strong engineering study on data curation for small code pretraining, but its core insight (distributional alignment/data quality) is less novel and more domain/scale-specific, with some confounding and reproducibility constraints.

Paper 2 (LPW/SLPW) demonstrates higher scientific impact due to its superior empirical results on modern, challenging benchmarks and its relevance to current state-of-the-art LLM usage. While Paper 1 addresses a fundamental training issue (aligning LLMs with code structure via RL), its evaluation is limited to outdated model sizes (GPT-2 era) and smaller benchmarks. Paper 2, conversely, achieves state-of-the-art performance on difficult datasets like APPS and CodeContests using contemporary models (GPT-4, Llama-3). Its core contribution—'plan verification' as a natural language oracle for debugging—is a highly effective prompting strategy that bridges the gap between reasoning and coding. This workflow-oriented approach is more immediately applicable and impactful for the current generation of frozen, large-scale models compared to the expensive re-training proposed in Paper 1.

Spider 2.0 is a landmark benchmark contribution that fundamentally redefines text-to-SQL evaluation by exposing a massive gap between academic benchmark performance (91.2% on Spider 1.0) and real enterprise requirements (21.3% on Spider 2.0). It provides years of research headroom, actionable diagnostic insights, and directly addresses the critical disconnect between research demonstrations and production deployments. Its impact spans text-to-SQL, code agents, and enterprise AI. In contrast, PLUM is a solid but incremental engineering contribution that combines existing techniques (DPO/KTO, on-policy sampling, LLM-generated test cases) with modest absolute improvements on standard benchmarks. While practical, PLUM's novelty is limited and its long-term impact will diminish as preference learning becomes standard in code model training. Spider 2.0 will catalyze far more downstream research and has broader cross-field influence.

While both papers address significant efficiency bottlenecks in LLM deployment, Paper 1 (FLoRA) introduces a more fundamental architectural modification with broader potential impact. FLoRA tackles the 'multi-tenant serving' problem—serving personalized models to thousands of users simultaneously—which is a critical barrier for the commercial viability of fine-tuned LLMs. Its solution enables heterogeneous batching without the heavy compute cost of standard methods. Although Paper 2 (PIE) offers a clever optimization for the specific use case of code editing (adjusting RoPE embeddings), it is a more narrow, application-specific engineering fix. FLoRA's impact extends across all domains requiring personalized adaptation (not just code), making it a more significant contribution to the general infrastructure of foundation model serving.

Paper 1 (IdentityChain) introduces a fundamental new dimension for evaluating Code LLMs—self-consistency—that goes beyond simple accuracy. By formalizing the relationship between code generation and summarization as a cycle of semantic preservation, it addresses a deeper theoretical gap in how we understand model robustness and 'understanding'. The TOM score and the relaxation of semantic equality are methodologically innovative contributions that are applicable to a wide range of problems beyond the paper's immediate scope. While Paper 2 (GitChameleon) addresses a very practical and timely issue (library versioning), its impact is constrained by the small size of its dataset (116 samples) and its narrower focus on specific library constraints rather than fundamental model capabilities. Experts are likely to view Paper 1's framework as a more significant, generalizable advancement in the science of evaluating generative AI.

Paper 1 (ChartMimic) is likely to have higher scientific impact due to its stronger novelty/innovation and broader cross-field relevance: it benchmarks a realistic multimodal task (chart image + instruction → executable rendering code) that stresses visual understanding, code generation, and cross-modal reasoning simultaneously. Its scale/diversity (4,800 curated triplets across many chart types/domains) and its methodological contribution (multi-level evaluation with execution-based tracing plus holistic scoring) make it a robust, reusable community benchmark. Applications extend beyond code LLMs to scientific communication, visualization tooling, and multimodal reasoning research. Paper 2 targets an important real-world issue (version-aware code) with rigorous execution-based tests, but its small dataset (116) constrains benchmarking authority and breadth. Overall, ChartMimic offers a larger, more general platform likely to shape model evaluation and development more widely.

Paper 2 (LPW/SLPW) demonstrates substantially higher scientific impact potential due to its strong empirical results across six benchmarks and five LLM backbones, achieving state-of-the-art performance with a well-motivated and reproducible workflow. The plan verification mechanism is a clean conceptual contribution with measurable gains, and the comprehensive experimental evaluation (ablations, cost analysis, failure categorization, difficulty stratification) reflects strong methodological rigor. Paper 1, while conceptually ambitious in proposing neurally compiled libraries for LLMs, presents preliminary and largely unconvincing experimental results (35% sorting accuracy, no baselines, no compositionality demonstrations at scale). Its key contributions remain aspirational rather than validated, and acknowledged scalability barriers undermine its near-term impact. Paper 2's practical applicability to AI-assisted programming tools and its demonstrated improvements on challenging competitive programming benchmarks give it broader and more immediate real-world relevance.

Paper 1 has higher likely scientific impact due to a concrete, novel inference-time method (SFS) that measurably improves LLM code generation across multiple established benchmarks with ablations and some theoretical grounding. It is immediately deployable (no training/data), aligns with the timely test-time compute scaling trend, and its core “diversify via intermediate improvement directions” idea plausibly transfers beyond coding to other search-heavy LLM tasks, giving broader field impact. Paper 2’s motivating idea (OOD formal systems to test generalization) is reasonable but the contribution is largely conceptual and under-executed: no VM/tooling or reusable benchmark is delivered, evaluation is manual and anecdotal with tiny scope, and the proposed computational model appears superficial. Thus experts would view Paper 1 as more rigorous, actionable, and broadly influential.

CodeIt makes a stronger scientific contribution by addressing a well-established, challenging benchmark (ARC) with a clean algorithmic approach—iterative policy-guided synthesis with hindsight relabeling—that demonstrates inter-task generalization and achieves state-of-the-art results competitive with GPT-4. The hindsight relabeling technique is a transferable insight applicable beyond ARC. In contrast, COOL lacks any external baselines, evaluates only on simple toy domains, and its dramatic improvements largely stem from encoding expert domain knowledge into the DSL structure rather than advancing synthesis algorithms. CodeIt's positioning within the broader landscape of self-improving LLMs (STaR, ReST) and abstract reasoning is timely and well-contextualized, while COOL's narrow evaluation and overclaimed generality significantly limit its potential influence on the field.

While both papers address critical issues in code LLMs, Paper 1 (PIE) tackles the fundamental problem of software efficiency in a post-Moore's law era with higher methodological rigor. Its introduction of a gem5-based simulation environment sets a new standard for reproducible performance evaluation, addressing a pervasive crisis of reproducibility in systems research. The demonstrated speedups (6.86x) are substantial and the techniques (performance-conditioned generation, self-play) are transferable. Paper 2 identifies an important problem (API updates) and creates a solid benchmark, but it primarily establishes that existing methods fail rather than offering a strong solution. Paper 1's combination of a massive dataset, rigorous evaluation infrastructure, and effective new methodology gives it broader immediate utility and higher potential to influence both ML and Systems communities.

Paper 2 has higher likely scientific impact because it contributes durable research infrastructure (a 77K+ edit dataset plus a gem5-based deterministic evaluation protocol) that can become a community benchmark, enabling reproducible comparisons and follow-on work across NLP, PL, and systems. Its methodology directly addresses a known but underappreciated confound (noisy performance measurement), with extensive simulations and comprehensive strategy sweeps, improving rigor and credibility. The applications—LLM-driven high-level performance optimization—are broadly valuable and timely given software-centric performance gains post–Moore’s law. Paper 1 is relevant and useful, but the core idea (evolutionary search wrapping LLM fuzzing) is more incremental, the empirical gains are modest and partly confounded by differing LLM backbones, and its impact is narrower (DL-library fuzzing) with weaker standard-setting potential.

Paper 1 (PIE) presents a mathematically elegant, practically impactful solution to a well-defined problem in real-time code editing with LLMs. It offers rigorous experiments across multiple model scales, languages, and editing scenarios, demonstrating >85% latency reduction with minimal accuracy loss. The method is immediately deployable in production coding assistants using RoPE-based models, requires no retraining, and opens a new research direction on dynamic KV cache management. Paper 2, while raising a reasonable question about testing AI generalization on novel formal systems, is fundamentally incomplete: it lacks automated evaluation infrastructure, presents only three anecdotal case studies without statistical rigor, and does not deliver on its promised contributions (no VM, no benchmark, no improvement mechanism). Paper 1's combination of theoretical clarity, comprehensive evaluation, and immediate practical applicability gives it vastly higher scientific impact.

Paper 1 (CADCodeVerify) addresses a highly timely and growing intersection of Generative AI and Manufacturing/3D Design. It introduces a specific, reproducible benchmark (CADPrompt) for an under-resourced task, which is a significant contribution that facilitates future research. Its methodology, leveraging Vision-Language Models for self-correction, aligns with current trends in multimodal agentic workflows. In contrast, Paper 2 (COOL) suffers from a critical methodological flaw: it lacks comparisons against external state-of-the-art baselines, comparing only against itself. Furthermore, its improvements rely heavily on manually encoding expert domain knowledge into the DSL, limiting its scalability and generalizability compared to the more universal approach of Paper 1.

Paper 1 is likely to have higher scientific impact because it delivers both a scalable training framework (CodeFavor) and a rigorously curated, verifiable benchmark (CodePrefBench) that can become shared infrastructure for evaluating and aligning code LLMs across correctness/efficiency/security and human taste. Its synthetic-evolution data recipe directly targets a major deployment bottleneck—preference data cost and reliability—and the empirical finding that human preference is often unreliable for non-functional properties is broadly relevant to RLHF/DPO practice. Methodologically, it offers extensive ablations, contamination checks, and oracle-based labeling, and it is reproducible with open models/data. Paper 2 is novel and timely but narrower (straight-line code, single closed model dependency) and is less likely to generalize into a widely adopted community benchmark or alignment pipeline.

CodeChain demonstrates substantially higher scientific impact through its methodological rigor, strong empirical results (35% improvement on APPS, 76% on CodeContests), comprehensive ablation studies, and immediate practical applicability as a training-free inference framework. It was accepted at ICLR 2024 and addresses a well-defined problem with a complete, validated solution. Paper 2, while conceptually interesting in proposing neurally compiled libraries for LLM augmentation, presents preliminary experiments with weak results (35% sorting accuracy), lacks baselines, and fails to demonstrate its key claimed capability (compositionality). Its core contributions remain aspirational rather than validated, making it closer to a position paper than a complete research contribution. CodeChain's combination of novelty, thoroughness, and demonstrated effectiveness positions it for significantly greater adoption and influence.

While both papers address important gaps in code generation evaluation, Paper 2 (ML-Bench) offers a more substantial contribution due to its scale and scope. Paper 1 (GitChameleon) identifies a critical issue (version mismatch) but relies on a very small dataset (116 samples), limiting its statistical power and breadth. Paper 2 provides a much larger dataset (9,641 examples) and addresses a broader, highly relevant problem: 'repository-level' interactions, including environment setup and agentic workflows. ML-Bench's dual setup (code generation vs. agentic execution) and integration with popular agent frameworks make it a more robust and timely platform for evaluating the next generation of coding agents, whereas GitChameleon serves more as a niche diagnostic tool.

Paper 1 (WebAgent/HTML-T5) has higher likely scientific impact because it introduces a domain-specialized model (HTML-T5) with clear architectural inductive biases and pretraining objectives for structured HTML—an advance that can generalize to other structured-document modalities and influence model design beyond web agents. It also demonstrates large, practical gains on real-world web automation and multiple established benchmarks (MiniWoB++, Mind2Web), making it timely for agentic AI and web interaction. Methodologically, it provides substantial ablations and cross-benchmark validation, and contributes both a system recipe and a reusable model class. Paper 2 (DyLAN) is a useful framework for dynamic multi-agent collaboration, but its novelty is more incremental (agent pruning/selection and a heuristic importance score), results are tightly coupled to proprietary APIs, and the improvements may be overtaken quickly by newer base models, limiting lasting cross-field impact.

BigCodeBench (Paper 1) has higher potential impact because it addresses the central bottleneck in the field of code generation: the saturation of algorithmic benchmarks like HumanEval. By introducing a massive, rigorous, execution-based benchmark for *practical* library usage and complex instruction following, it provides the immediate "next step" standard for evaluating general-purpose coding agents. While SecCodePLT (Paper 2) makes a valuable contribution to security evaluation, it is a niche subset of the broader code generation problem. BigCodeBench's scale, methodological rigor (99% branch coverage), and findings on "model laziness" give it broader relevance to the core development of future LLMs, making it likely to become a primary citation in nearly every subsequent paper on code generation capabilities.

Paper 2 has higher potential scientific impact because it introduces a new, general evaluation dimension (formal self-consistency across NL↔code transformations) that challenges the community’s prevailing accuracy-centric paradigm and can influence model assessment, debugging, and trustworthiness research beyond coding. IdentityChain is methodologically crisp (formal definitions, efficient chaining, execution-based TOM metric with human-correlation validation) and is broadly extensible to other paired-modality domains, increasing cross-field relevance. Paper 1 is timely and practically useful as a repository-level benchmark for ML agent workflows, but its scientific novelty is more incremental (benchmark construction), with impact constrained by potential training-data contamination, relatively small agent evaluation sample, and narrower domain scope (18 ML repos, many tasks akin to CLI/template filling).

While both papers are strong, ChartMimic (Paper 2) is likely to have higher impact due to its introduction of a high-quality, diverse benchmark in a rapidly growing area (multimodal code generation). The field currently lacks rigorous evaluation standards for cross-modal reasoning tasks involving complex visual data like charts. By providing a large-scale dataset (4,800 triplets) and a novel evaluation methodology (code-tracing metrics), ChartMimic enables future research and model comparison in a way that a specific method like MGDebugger (Paper 1) does not. MGDebugger proposes a clever technique, but its reliance on LLM-simulated execution raises questions about scalability and reliability that may limit widespread adoption compared to a foundational benchmark.

WebAgent makes stronger architectural and methodological contributions with broader impact. It introduces HTML-T5 (a novel domain-specialized LLM with structural inductive biases), demonstrates a principled modular agent architecture combining specialized smaller models with large general-purpose ones, and achieves substantial real-world improvements (50%+ on real websites, SoTA on multiple benchmarks). These contributions advance fundamental understanding of LLM agent design, domain-specific pretraining, and modular architectures — influencing the rapidly growing field of autonomous web agents. ChartMimic, while a well-constructed and timely benchmark, is primarily an evaluation resource for a relatively narrow task (matplotlib chart reproduction). Benchmarks have impact, but WebAgent's combined architectural innovation, systems design principles, and empirical demonstrations across multiple evaluation axes represent a higher-impact contribution that has shaped subsequent work on LLM-based agents.

Paper 2 presents a substantially more novel and technically ambitious contribution—augmenting LLMs with differentiable compiled algorithm libraries—addressing a fundamental limitation (LLMs' inability to perform true algorithmic reasoning) with a theoretically grounded approach rooted in complexity theory (TC⁰ bounds). While both papers have weak experimental execution and are characterized as preliminary/workshop-level, Paper 2 introduces genuinely new architectural ideas (neural compilation into differentiable libraries, bootstrapping strategy, call instructions for composition) that could influence future research on neuro-symbolic integration. Paper 1's 'novel computational model' is superficial (base-10 vs base-2 renaming) and documents known LLM failures without proposing any improvement mechanism. Paper 2's conceptual framework—even if incompletely validated—opens a richer research direction with broader potential impact across reasoning, planning, and algorithmic tasks.

Paper 1 (L2MAC) has higher impact potential due to a clearer, more novel systems contribution: a practical, general-purpose stored-program/multi-agent architecture enabling long-horizon, multi-file code generation beyond context-window limits. It demonstrates strong empirical gains on realistic system-design coding tasks, includes diagnostic/ablation-style analyses (context management, read/write behavior, verification), and partially addresses evaluation bias with human validation—supporting methodological rigor and reproducibility. Its applications (software engineering automation, robust agentic workflows with persistent mutable memory) are immediate and broadly extensible beyond coding, making it timely and likely to influence follow-on agent/memory research. Paper 2 targets an important area (cross-lingual/domain adaptation) but appears to repackage known theory, lacks implementation specificity and theory–practice linkage, shows weak/misleading empirical improvements, and provides insufficient statistical reporting, limiting credibility and downstream adoption.

Paper 2 (EASE) presents a more complete and validated contribution with systematic experiments on established benchmarks, clear ablation studies, and demonstrated practical advantages (90%+ reduction in inference cost). While Paper 1 proposes a more ambitious conceptual direction, its experimental results are weak (35% sorting accuracy), lacks baselines, and key claims (compositionality) remain unvalidated. Paper 2's paradigm of compiling LLM knowledge into deterministic programs has broader applicability across embodied AI and robotics, and its interpretability guarantees address a genuine need. Paper 1 is acknowledged even by its own assessment as more of a position/workshop paper than a complete research contribution, whereas Paper 2 delivers concrete, reproducible results with practical significance despite its incremental nature.

While Paper 1 (LPW) offers a solid workflow improvement for competitive programming tasks (short, algorithmic snippets), Paper 2 (L2MAC) addresses a far more fundamental and commercially relevant problem: generating large, consistent, multi-file codebases (system design). L2MAC's architectural innovation—applying a von Neumann structure with a read/write file store to overcome context window limitations—is a significant conceptual leap beyond standard prompting strategies. The ability to maintain consistency across a large codebase via a control unit and observable memory is a crucial step toward autonomous software engineering. Furthermore, L2MAC's applicability to broader long-context generation tasks (like book writing) suggests wider cross-field utility than LPW's specific focus on test-driven algorithmic problems.

Paper 1 is more likely to have higher scientific impact because it contributes both a reusable evaluation framework (a taxonomy/meta-benchmark for compositional generalization in program synthesis) and a novel method (execution-subgoal decomposition) that consistently improves performance across two distinct synthesis domains, with ablations and controlled dataset construction supporting methodological rigor. Its core idea—predicting intermediate execution states to enable stepwise synthesis—generalizes conceptually to broader reasoning/planning in code generation and beyond, and it directly informs LLM prompting strategies. Paper 2 is highly timely and valuable as a benchmark audit, but its impact is narrower (primarily recalibrating one benchmark), and its proposed SWE-bench+ still suffers from weak-test issues and low statistical power due to very few solved instances, which may limit long-term adoption and cross-field influence.

Paper 2 (STOP) is more novel and likely higher-impact scientifically because it opens a new research direction: recursive self-improvement of LM scaffolding code (meta-optimization), connecting modern agent/scaffolding work to classic RSI ideas. Its implications extend beyond code generation to automated algorithm/scaffold design, optimization, and AI safety. The inclusion of sandbox-circumvention/specification-gaming measurements provides timely, policy-relevant evidence and a methodological template for evaluating self-improving systems. While empirical scale and baselines are limited, the conceptual contribution and breadth across fields (ML systems, optimization, automated programming, alignment/safety) are larger than Paper 1’s more incremental—but rigorous and practically useful—workflow improvement for test-driven code generation, whose impact is narrower and more dependent on visible tests and prompt engineering.

CodeDPO addresses a broader and more impactful problem—aligning code generation models via preference optimization—with extensive evaluation across 8 models and 5+ benchmarks. Its PageRank-inspired self-validation mechanism for constructing preference datasets without external dependencies is a scalable, practical contribution applicable to the rapidly growing code LLM ecosystem. The framework directly improves both correctness and efficiency, two universally valued properties. Paper 2 presents an elegant within-prompt search idea but is constrained to straight-line code only, depends on a single closed-source frontier model (gpt-4o), and addresses a narrower programming-by-example setting. CodeDPO's broader applicability across models, its training-time contribution (reusable dataset + methodology), and its relevance to the mainstream code LLM alignment pipeline give it higher potential for widespread adoption and citation impact.

ExeDec offers a more fundamental scientific contribution through its meta-benchmark for compositional generalization, which addresses a core theoretical gap in AI reasoning. While EvAFuzz presents a practical engineering improvement for software testing (finding 2 more bugs than SOTA), ExeDec establishes a new evaluation framework that can be applied across many future synthesis models. The rigorous taxonomy of generalization types (length, concept composition, etc.) and the insight of using execution states as subgoals provide deeper intellectual value to the field than EvAFuzz's application of evolutionary algorithms to prompt engineering, which is a more incremental methodological step.

Paper 1 likely has higher scientific impact because it proposes a new algorithmic direction—value-based RL for LLM program synthesis—challenging a field-wide default (policy gradients) with principled mechanisms (LM-logit Q initialization, conservative Bellman operator) and transferable ideas for other verifiable-sequence domains. Its contributions can influence RL+LLM training methodology broadly, not just code, and introduce a practical “free” reward/ranking signal that addresses deployment without tests. Paper 2 is valuable infrastructure (a domain benchmark) with clear utility for bioinformatics, but benchmarks typically have narrower conceptual spillover and its methodology has potential evaluation bias (GPT-authored summaries) and limited cross-language usefulness (Java near-zero). Overall, Paper 1 is more innovative and field-shaping.

LILO makes a more fundamental and cross-disciplinary contribution by introducing a neurosymbolic framework that addresses the deeper problem of automated library learning and code refactoring. Its key insight—that auto-documentation bridges the gap between symbolic compression and LLM-based synthesis—is surprising, broadly applicable, and connects to multiple research communities (program synthesis, neurosymbolic AI, cognitive science, software engineering). Paper 2 (LPW) is a solid engineering contribution with strong empirical results, but it primarily introduces a structured prompting workflow for code generation that is incremental over prior chain-of-thought and self-debugging approaches. LPW's reliance on prompt engineering, visible test cases, and single-function benchmarks limits its generalizability. LILO's modular architecture, the negative result on LLM compression, and the demonstration that interpretability is performance-critical represent more lasting conceptual contributions that are likely to influence subsequent neurosymbolic system design.

While Paper 1 offers a solid practical recipe for data-efficient code pretraining, Paper 2 (ChartMimic) makes a more significant contribution to the field of multimodal evaluation. ChartMimic introduces a novel, rigorous benchmark for a complex cross-modal task (chart-to-code) that is currently underexplored. Its methodology—specifically the code-tracing evaluation metrics and the realistic 'Direct vs. Customized' task framing—addresses a critical gap in evaluating LMMs beyond simple VQA. The benchmark's scale (4.8k examples) and the clear differentiation it provides between open-weight and proprietary models give it high utility for future research. In contrast, Paper 1's findings on data quality (alignment with downstream tasks) are valuable but conceptually incremental, effectively confirming known principles of domain adaptation without offering a fundamental new paradigm.

Paper 1 likely has higher scientific impact because it introduces a broadly useful, low-cost benchmark (FeatEng) for evaluating LLMs on an integrated, open-ended data-science task, with substantial empirical scope (103 datasets, 23+ models, 30 runs) and strong evidence of external validity via high correlation with Chatbot Arena. Its applicability spans LLM evaluation, AutoML, and practical ML workflow tooling, making its impact more cross-cutting and timely as traditional benchmarks saturate. Paper 2 targets an important niche (DL library fuzzing) but appears more incremental: modest crash gains, confounded baseline comparisons due to differing LLM backbones, unclear cost/rigor (no multi-run significance), and delayed reproducibility. Experts would view FeatEng as more generalizable and likely to be adopted as community infrastructure.

WebAgent makes broader and more significant contributions across multiple dimensions. It introduces both a novel architecture (HTML-T5 with domain-specific pre-training) and a complete modular agent system, demonstrating 50%+ improvements on real-world web tasks. The work advances fundamental understanding of how to design domain-specialized LLMs with structural inductive biases, providing a generalizable template beyond web automation. It achieves SoTA on multiple established benchmarks (MiniWoB++, Mind2Web) and demonstrates real-world applicability. Published at ICLR 2024, it shaped the rapidly growing LLM agent field. In contrast, GitChameleon is a well-executed but narrower diagnostic benchmark contribution with only 116 samples, limited to Python libraries, and without exploring mitigation strategies. While it identifies an important problem, its impact is constrained by scale and scope compared to WebAgent's architectural, methodological, and systems-level contributions.

While ML-Bench (Paper 1) offers a useful benchmark for a practical problem, LILO (Paper 2) presents a more fundamental scientific advance in neurosymbolic AI. LILO solves a critical interface problem between symbolic systems and LLMs: the finding that 'anonymous abstractions act as obfuscation' and that auto-documentation restores performance is a novel, generalizable insight into how neural and symbolic components must interact. Paper 2 also demonstrates dramatic efficiency gains over prior art (DreamCoder), reducing computational costs from months to hours. In contrast, Paper 1 is primarily an evaluation framework whose long-term value is constrained by the rapid obsolescence of benchmarks and reliance on specific, potentially contaminated repositories.

Paper 2 presents a complete, methodologically sound contribution to a critical problem in AI code generation (reducing executable but incorrect code). It proposes a novel fine-tuning strategy using execution-derived specifications, validates it on standard benchmarks (Arcade, DS-1000), and offers actionable insights about data abstraction levels. In contrast, Paper 1 is an early-stage concept with severe methodological flaws: it relies on manual evaluation of only three problems, fails to build the virtual machine it proposes, and lacks statistical rigor. While Paper 2 suffers from reproducibility issues due to proprietary models, its rigorous experimental design and clear evidence of utility make it significantly more impactful than the anecdotal and incomplete work in Paper 1.

While Paper 1 provides a valuable critique of a popular benchmark (SWE-bench) and highlights important issues like data leakage and weak tests, Paper 2 offers a more fundamental scientific contribution. Paper 2 systematically investigates the nature of abstract reasoning in AI, providing a controlled comparison between inductive and transductive learning. Its finding that these approaches are fundamentally complementary—handling distinct types of reasoning (symbolic/precise vs. perceptual/fuzzy)—provides deep insight into neurosymbolic AI architecture. Furthermore, Paper 2 proposes a transferable methodology for synthetic data generation and achieves near-human performance on the notoriously difficult ARC benchmark. Paper 1 is excellent maintenance work for the field, but Paper 2 advances our theoretical understanding of how to build reasoning systems.

Paper 2 likely has higher impact because it opens and standardizes an under-benchmarked, high-value application area (natural-language-to-parametric CAD for manufacturing) with a concrete benchmark (CADPrompt) and a reusable verification/refinement paradigm using VLM-based question generation and visual inspection. Its contributions are directly actionable for real-world design workflows and extend beyond CAD to other visually-grounded code/specification tasks. While gains are modest and the dataset is small, the combination of benchmark + iterative self-verification loop is a timely, cross-cutting systems+method contribution. Paper 1’s RACE is useful and timely for code-LLM evaluation, especially for contamination resistance, but it is more incremental (recombining established proxies like MI/style metrics) and narrower in scope (Python-only, mostly surface-level quality signals), limiting breadth versus Paper 2’s manufacturing and multimodal reasoning reach.

Paper 1 (SFS) has higher estimated scientific impact for several reasons: (1) Its core insight—prompting LLMs to generate diverse textual 'directions' before code generation improves exploration—is simple, broadly transferable beyond code to any reasoning domain using inference-time search, and immediately deployable without training. (2) It addresses the highly timely topic of inference-time compute scaling with strong empirical results across five benchmarks. (3) The method is parameter-free and model-agnostic, lowering adoption barriers. (4) The finding that even nonsensical prompts improve diversity is a striking insight about LLM behavior with broad implications. Paper 2 (CodeIt), while methodologically clean, is tightly coupled to a hand-designed DSL for ARC, achieves modest absolute performance (12%), and shows only marginal improvement over simpler mutation baselines (48 vs 41/400), limiting its broader applicability and transformative potential.

Paper 2 (RACE) likely has higher scientific impact because it proposes a broadly useful evaluation framework that can reshape how the community measures progress: moving beyond pass@k correctness to multi-dimensional, demand-dependent code quality, and empirically demonstrating contamination resistance via a controlled finetuning experiment. Benchmarks often have outsized cross-field influence (model training, alignment, software engineering, robustness), and RACE’s promptable requirements make memorization less effective—highly timely given widespread benchmark saturation and leakage concerns. While its metrics are imperfect and Python-only, the core idea is extensible and directly actionable for both research and industry. Paper 1 is a strong engineering study on data curation for small code models, but its novelty is more integrative, its “quality=distributional alignment” insight is less surprising, and reliance on proprietary data/large teacher models may limit reproducibility and broader uptake.

While Paper 1 presents a novel algorithmic approach (value-based RL for code), Paper 2 addresses a fundamental and neglected problem in the field: the evaluation of code efficiency. Paper 2's methodological rigor is superior, offering a statistically grounded metric (eff@k) that solves specific flaws in prior benchmarking (like handling censored data). Benchmarks and robust evaluation metrics often have a longer-lasting and broader impact on a field than specific algorithmic improvements, especially when they reveal significant capability gaps (as Paper 2 does regarding LLM efficiency vs. correctness). Paper 2 is likely to become a standard tool for future research, whereas Paper 1 is an exploratory step in a specific sub-direction.

Paper 2 presents a more novel and impactful contribution: using LLMs as both policy and implicit value function within a single expanding prompt for program synthesis, combining execution feedback with search in a training-free, domain-agnostic manner. It is evaluated across five benchmarks spanning multiple domains, compared against meaningful baselines (including Tree-of-Thought), and includes insightful behavioral analyses. Paper 1, while proposing a reasonable engineering framework (CoL + NNFC), suffers from critical weaknesses: no comparison against any external baseline, evaluation on only two narrow/simple domains, and improvements that largely stem from encoding expert knowledge rather than advancing synthesis algorithms. Paper 2's conceptual elegance, broader applicability, methodological rigor, and relevance to the active LLM reasoning community give it substantially higher potential for scientific impact and influence on subsequent research.

Paper 1 has higher potential scientific impact because it proposes a broadly generalizable, tool-integrated paradigm (“LLM as formalizer + formal solver”) that advances capabilities on hard combinatorial planning/optimization across diverse domains, with strong empirical validation (9 tasks, ablations, robustness via obfuscated predicates, comparisons to strong baselines). Its applications span operations research, scheduling, and robotics-style task planning, and it contributes an architectural template likely to be reused and extended (e.g., to MILP/CP solvers). Paper 2 is timely and useful as an evaluation benchmark, but its novelty is more incremental (composition of existing metrics) and its scope is narrower (Python-only, proxy quality measures with debated validity), which may limit cross-field breadth and long-term influence compared to a general planning methodology.

While Paper 2 addresses a specific, practical domain (CAD) with a solid benchmark contribution, Paper 1 (DyLAN) proposes a fundamental architectural innovation for the broader field of multi-agent systems. The concept of 'Agent Importance Score' as an unsupervised metric for pruning and optimizing agent teams is a methodological advance that transcends specific tasks. This generalizable framework for optimizing agent collaboration addresses a critical bottleneck in the rapidly expanding area of Agentic AI. The broader applicability of DyLAN to reasoning, coding, and general knowledge tasks suggests it will likely garner more citations and influence future system designs more significantly than the domain-specific improvements offered by Paper 2.

Paper 1 (SFS) is likely to have higher scientific impact because it directly addresses the critical and timely problem of 'inference-time scaling'—how to trade compute for intelligence during generation. Its proposed method (SFS) offers a practical, model-agnostic solution that significantly boosts state-of-the-art performance (8.6% on HumanEval+) using a novel mechanism ('scattering' via textual directions). This connects deeply with current trends in 'system 2' thinking for LLMs (similar to OpenAI o1). While Paper 2 offers a valuable methodological insight about measuring diversity (decoupling semantic vs. syntactic diversity), its findings are essentially a measurement critique with less direct utility for improving future models. Paper 1 provides a building block for next-generation inference strategies, giving it broader application potential and immediate relevance to the engineering of high-performance reasoning systems.

Paper 1 is likely to have higher scientific impact because it introduces a more conceptually novel and broadly extensible evaluation principle—formal self-consistency via round-trip NL↔code transformations—rather than primarily composing existing quality proxies. IdentityChain targets a fundamental property (semantic coherence across modalities/tasks) that can generalize beyond Python and beyond code (e.g., other paired-generation domains), potentially shaping evaluation theory and model-debugging practice. Methodologically, it provides a clear formal definition and an execution-based metric (TOM) with validated correlation to human judgments, and it yields distinct, surprising insights (self-consistency decouples from accuracy) plus actionable debugging signals. Paper 2 is timely and practically relevant, especially for contamination resistance, but relies on more debatable/shallower proxies (MI, style constraints, runtime heuristics) and is more incremental, likely limiting cross-field influence.

Spider 2.0 has higher estimated scientific impact for several reasons. First, it addresses a critical and timely gap: existing text-to-SQL benchmarks (Spider 1.0, BIRD) are approaching saturation, and Spider 2.0 resets the evaluation landscape with enterprise-scale complexity that will drive years of research. The dramatic performance drop (91.2% → 21.3%) provides a compelling and widely citable result. Second, its breadth of impact is larger—it influences text-to-SQL, code agents, enterprise AI, and benchmark design methodology across NLP, databases, and software engineering communities. Third, its practical relevance to industry (real cloud databases, multiple SQL dialects, production-scale data) means progress directly translates to enterprise deployments, attracting both academic and industrial attention. LILO makes a solid neurosymbolic contribution with the clever AutoDoc insight, but its impact is more niche—limited to program synthesis and library learning—with smaller-scale experiments, higher variance, and reliance on deprecated APIs. Spider 2.0's scale, timeliness, and community-wide relevance give it substantially greater impact potential.

SWE-Bench+ addresses a critically important and timely problem—auditing the validity of the most influential benchmark for LLM coding agents. Its findings that ~64% of successful patches are problematic (due to solution leakage or weak tests) directly recalibrates the field's understanding of LLM capabilities, affecting industry claims and research directions. Despite methodological limitations (no inter-rater reliability metrics, small sample sizes on SWE-bench+), the paper's breadth of impact is substantially greater: it influences how the entire LLM-for-SE community evaluates progress. Paper 1 (COOL), while presenting interesting engineering ideas for DSL-based synthesis, suffers from fatal evaluation weaknesses—no external baselines, narrow toy domains, and improvements that largely stem from encoding expert knowledge rather than advancing synthesis algorithms. SWE-Bench+'s meta-evaluative contribution affects a much larger research community and addresses a more consequential question.

L2MAC presents a more novel and impactful contribution: a principled von Neumann architecture framework for LLM-based multi-agent systems that addresses the fundamental context window limitation for long output generation. It introduces new architectural concepts (instruction registry, file store with read/write access, control unit with verification), demonstrates large empirical margins over strong baselines (59-92% vs 10-53%), and establishes design principles (P1-P3) applicable beyond code generation. The framework's influence on subsequent multi-agent coding systems and its broader applicability to any long-output generation task give it wider impact. RACE, while addressing a real gap in code evaluation, primarily composes existing metrics (MI, AST analysis, runtime monitoring) into a benchmark with relatively surface-level quality proxies. L2MAC's systems-level innovation and strong empirical results represent a more significant advance than RACE's incremental improvement in evaluation methodology.

Paper 1 (LILO) presents a more significant methodological advance by successfully integrating Large Language Models with symbolic compression in a way that solves a known failure mode (the opacity of symbolic abstractions). Its 'AutoDoc' mechanism—using LLMs to document symbolic code so other LLM calls can understand it—is a novel, generalizable insight that bridges the neuro-symbolic gap. This has broad implications for software engineering, program synthesis, and agentic AI. While Paper 2 offers a rigorous empirical study with a useful counter-narrative about diversity, its impact is narrower (focused on evaluation metrics) and its core finding is partially undermined by its own analysis showing gains are driven mostly by basic code coherence rather than true semantic variety. Paper 1 proposes a new generative framework, whereas Paper 2 offers a measurement observation.

Paper 1 (WebAgent) represents a substantially more impactful contribution across all evaluation dimensions. It introduces a novel modular architecture combining specialized LLMs (HTML-T5 with domain-specific pre-training) for real-world web automation, achieving 50%+ improvement on real websites and state-of-the-art on multiple benchmarks (MiniWoB++, Mind2Web). Its contributions span architectural innovation, domain-specific pre-training strategies, and practical system design for LLM agents. Published at ICLR 2024, it addresses a critical gap in the rapidly growing LLM agent field. Paper 2 presents an incremental prompting technique (print debugging for LLMs) evaluated on only ~200 LeetCode problems with a single model, no statistical testing, and marginal improvements. Its contribution amounts to an engineering trick rather than a fundamental advance, with insufficient experimental rigor to support its claims.

Paper 2 has higher likely scientific impact due to a clearer, more novel and broadly adoptable technical contribution (PIE) that directly improves inference efficiency for RoPE-based LLMs in a realistic, high-frequency setting (interactive code editing). It offers a mathematically grounded fix for KV-cache positional inconsistency with strong empirical validation across multiple model scales, tasks, and edit types, plus large measured latency gains and straightforward deployment (no retraining). This makes it timely and immediately useful for widely deployed coding assistants and potentially other interactive editing applications. Paper 1 targets an important robotics RL bottleneck, but its novelty is weakened by close concurrent work, and its evaluation is comparatively narrow (single sim environment), lacks strong baselines/defined metrics/statistical rigor, and depends heavily on proprietary LLM behavior, limiting confidence and near-term field-wide adoption.

Paper 1 (LILO) presents a complete, empirically validated framework that solves a critical problem in neurosymbolic AI: making symbolic abstractions interpretable to neural models. Its novelty (AutoDoc) is intuitively sound and rigorously tested, showing clear performance gains over state-of-the-art baselines like DreamCoder. The methodological rigor is high, with appropriate ablations and cost analyses. In contrast, Paper 2 is a preliminary exploration with weak experimental results (35% accuracy on sorting), missing baselines, and severe scalability issues acknowledged by the authors. While Paper 2's concept is interesting, Paper 1 delivers a working, impactful solution that immediately advances the field of program synthesis and library learning.

Paper 2 (LLMFP) has higher likely scientific impact due to a more novel and general architectural contribution (LLM as formalizer + solver) that cleanly addresses LLM planning limitations, strong methodological rigor (diverse 9-task suite, substantial query counts, multiple baselines across frontier models, ablations, robustness via predicate obfuscation), and broader real-world applicability (operations research, scheduling, logistics, and task planning). Its results directly inform an active debate on LLM reasoning vs. tool use, making it timely and field-shaping. Paper 1 targets an important robotics pain point, but the contribution appears less differentiated from concurrent reward-generation work and is weakened by unclear metrics, missing baselines, narrow simulation-only scope, and limited statistical/diagnostic evaluation, reducing confidence and breadth of impact.

Paper 1 addresses a fundamental and widely debated question in the field of Large Language Models: the 'mode collapse' effect of RLHF/instruction tuning. By challenging the prevailing narrative with a rigorous metric (code execution) that decouples surface-level diversity from semantic diversity, it offers a counter-intuitive finding that could reshape alignment methodologies across the entire field. While Paper 2 presents a solid engineering application in a niche domain (CAD) with incremental improvements, Paper 1 provides a methodological breakthrough in how we evaluate generative models generally. The insight that neural metrics negatively correlate with true semantic diversity invalidates common evaluation practices, giving Paper 1 significantly higher potential for broad, cross-disciplinary impact on future research.

Paper 1 has higher estimated scientific impact due to a more novel algorithmic shift and broader relevance: it introduces a principled value-based RL framework for program synthesis in a space dominated by policy gradients, with theoretically motivated components (LM-logit Q initialization, conservative Bellman operator with contraction guarantees) and a transferable idea of extracting a “free” reward model from learned value functions. Its contributions generalize beyond code to other verifiable sequence-generation tasks, aligning with major RL-for-LLMs trends and leveraging abundant off-policy data—an important, timely research direction. Paper 2 is appealing for interpretability/cost via LLM-distilled symbolic programs, but its scope is narrower (single benchmark family, limited relation set, bounding-box-only constraints) and has heavier dependence on proprietary GPT-4o, weakening reproducibility and limiting near-term field-wide uptake.

MathCoder has substantially higher scientific impact due to several factors: (1) It established the influential LCE (Language-Code-Execution) paradigm for interleaving natural language reasoning with code execution in open-source LLMs, which was widely adopted by subsequent works (OpenMathInstruct, DeepSeek-Math, etc.). (2) It was published at ICLR 2024 at a critical moment when the community needed open-source alternatives to GPT-4 Code Interpreter. (3) Its results were striking—achieving state-of-the-art on MATH and GSM8K, with a 7B model outperforming 70B alternatives. (4) Its contributions (dataset generation via problem interpolation, special-token training pipeline, execution-integrated inference) are broadly generalizable beyond mathematics. By contrast, SPA offers a clean but modest inference-time improvement (up to 7.6% Pass@1) with limited generalizability (model-specific tuning, open-source only, weaker Pass@10 results) and narrower methodological influence.

FLoRA addresses a fundamental systems-level bottleneck in multi-tenant LLM serving that affects the entire foundation model deployment ecosystem. Its elegant algebraic reformulation enabling batched heterogeneous adapter inference is broadly applicable across modalities (language, speech, code) and model architectures. Published at ICLR 2024, it has immediate practical relevance as personalized LLM serving scales globally. While Paper 1 (SPA) makes a solid contribution to code generation with attention dilution insights, its impact is narrower—restricted to open-source models, code generation tasks, and shows inconsistent improvements on diversity metrics. FLoRA's infrastructure-level contribution has broader downstream impact: it enables an entire class of serving scenarios rather than improving a specific task metric. The simplicity of implementation and clear practitioner guidance (when FLoRA beats LoRA) further enhances adoption potential.

Paper 1 (BigCodeBench) has higher likely scientific impact because it delivers a large, rigorously tested, broadly useful benchmark (1,140 tasks; 139 libraries; high branch coverage) that can immediately become standard infrastructure for evaluating and improving code-generating LLMs and tool-use/compositional reasoning. Its scale, domain diversity, and execution-based evaluation enable widespread adoption across academia and industry, with clear real-world relevance to practical programming and agentic workflows; the “Complete vs Instruct” split also yields actionable insights (e.g., instruction sensitivity, prompt-length “laziness”) for model training and evaluation. Paper 2 is conceptually insightful and timely for alignment/diversity measurement, but its dataset is small (21 tasks), generalizability beyond code is uncertain, and the headline semantic-diversity gain is partly explained by coherence effects, reducing downstream breadth and immediate applicability.
