Paper 2 offers valuable mechanistic theory for multitask representation learning, but its impact is constrained by restrictive assumptions (two-layer CNNs, nonstandard activation, stylized noise model) and a larger theory–practice gap, making it less immediately transferable despite topical relevance.

Paper 2 (MEMENTO) likely has higher scientific impact because it targets a broader and more central problem—efficient inference-time adaptation for neural combinatorial optimization—spanning many industrially relevant domains. The memory-based, learned update rule without backprop at inference is a timely, broadly reusable paradigm (meta-learning/retrieval-style adaptation) that can influence sequential decision-making beyond routing. Its demonstrated composability with existing solvers and strong budget-sensitive results increase real-world adoption potential. While Paper 1 is novel and rigorous within graph visualization and offers useful GNN techniques, its primary application area is narrower and evaluation focuses mainly on stress; scalability and generalization remain somewhat limited. Overall, MEMENTO’s combination of generality, deployment relevance, and alignment with current inference-time optimization trends suggests higher cross-field and practical impact.

Paper 2 offers a significant theoretical breakthrough by reverse-engineering a popular, empirically-discovered optimizer (Lion) to reveal it solves a specific constrained optimization problem. This not only legitimizes a widely used tool but also creates a new unifying framework (Lion-K) that connects modern heuristics to classical optimization methods like Frank-Wolfe and Nesterov momentum. In contrast, Paper 1 is primarily observational; while it correctly identifies non-smoothness as a theoretical gap, it relies largely on known results from subgradient theory without offering constructive solutions or rigorous new proofs. Paper 2's potential to guide hyperparameter tuning and inspire new optimizer designs gives it much broader utility and impact.

Paper 2 demonstrates higher scientific impact for several reasons: (1) It addresses a critically timely problem—AI-generated content watermarking—at the exact moment when governments and industry are relying on watermarking for AI governance, giving it immediate policy relevance. (2) It provides a clean, reusable methodological framework (optimization-based adaptive attacks with surrogate keys) that sets a new evaluation standard, analogous to how Carlini & Wagner transformed adversarial robustness evaluation. (3) Its comprehensive experimental validation across five diverse watermarking methods on practical systems (Stable Diffusion) demonstrates broad applicability. (4) The negative result—all tested methods broken at negligible quality cost in under 1 GPU hour—has profound implications for the entire watermarking-as-regulation paradigm. In contrast, Paper 1, while intellectually interesting, suffers from restrictive theoretical assumptions (quadratic losses), limited small-scale experiments, high computational overhead for marginal gains, and no comparison with modern SAM variants on competitive benchmarks.

Paper 1 makes a more complete and immediately impactful contribution within its domain. It establishes a new paradigm (DRL-guided improvement over construction heuristics) for JSSP with strong empirical validation showing large margins over prior DRL methods across extensive benchmarks, supported by theoretical complexity guarantees and a novel message-passing evaluator with independent utility. The contribution is self-contained: the demonstrated improvements are real and immediate. Paper 2, while theoretically elegant, has a critical gap between its motivation (accelerating training of large models) and its demonstrations (modest-scale experiments without wall-clock speedups for Transformers, the dominant architecture). The unexplained γ hyperparameter for transformers undermines generality. Paper 1's extensibility as a platform for hybrid methods in scheduling optimization gives it stronger catalytic potential for follow-up work.

While both papers are methodologically sound and timely, Paper 1 (PABBO) has higher potential for immediate and widespread impact. It addresses a critical bottleneck in Preferential Bayesian Optimization—computational speed—by applying amortization, achieving order-of-magnitude speedups that unlock real-time human-in-the-loop applications (e.g., visual design, robotics). This practical enablement of a whole class of interactive applications gives it a broader reach than Paper 2. Paper 2 is theoretically strong and defines a novel game class relevant to robust combinatorial optimization, but its impact is currently constrained by significant scalability limitations (high training costs for even 50-node problems) and a narrower scope of immediate application compared to the general-purpose optimization tool proposed in Paper 1.

CoRe-GD offers broader impact across multiple communities (graph visualization, GNN methodology, graph transformers) with several transferable technical innovations (positional rewiring, latent replay buffers for deep GNNs). It provides comprehensive experimental validation across diverse datasets with state-of-the-art results, and its contributions are immediately applicable. Paper 1 addresses an important but narrower theoretical gap in matrix sensing with a clean preconditioning idea, but lacks end-to-end recovery experiments, has potentially loose bounds, and faces scalability concerns. CoRe-GD's publication at ICLR 2024 and its practical resolution of the scalability bottleneck for neural graph drawing position it for greater citation impact and downstream influence.

Paper 2 has higher likely scientific impact because it proposes a simple, broadly reusable scaling rule (Depth-μP) with a near-“standard practice” adoption pathway for training very deep residual nets, and it backs it with a strong conceptual framework: a classification of depth/update scalings and a new feature-diversity exponent tying scaling to nontrivial feature learning in the infinite-depth limit. This gives it wider cross-field relevance (scaling laws, optimization, theory of deep learning) and timeliness as depth scaling is a central unresolved scaling axis. While nonlinear rigor is incomplete and Transformer applicability is limited (block depth ≥2), the unifying theory + practical hyperparameter transfer results make it more likely to shape future research and tooling than Paper 1’s variance-optimal perturbations, whose key method (DAP) has a circular dependency on gradient knowledge and more context-specific benefits.

Paper 1 offers a stronger fundamental theoretical contribution with broader applicability. By proving global convergence guarantees for sparse optimization with mixed constraints—a problem where previously only local guarantees existed—it solves a significant open problem in optimization theory. Its introduction of the 'Constrained l0-Three-Point Lemma' provides a reusable mathematical tool that will likely influence future theoretical work in sparse optimization. In contrast, Paper 2 is a solid engineering application of RL to quantum architecture search, but it suffers from severe scalability issues (limited to 4 qubits under noise) and relies on combining existing techniques rather than introducing a fundamental breakthrough. Paper 1's theoretical rigor and the ubiquity of sparse optimization problems across data science give it higher long-term scientific impact potential.

Paper 1 addresses a critical, timely issue in AI safety and governance: the reliability of watermarking for generative models. Its demonstration that current methods are fundamentally fragile against optimization-based attacks challenges the prevailing technical strategy for AI provenance, influencing both future research directions and policy decisions. While Paper 2 offers a solid theoretical unification of optimization ODEs, its impact is largely restricted to a specific subfield of mathematical optimization and offers incremental improvements (constant-factor convergence rates) rather than the paradigm-shifting practical and methodological implications of Paper 1.

Paper 2 has higher potential scientific impact due to a more novel, broadly relevant conceptual contribution: it offers an analytically tractable explanation for critical learning periods—previously largely empirical—showing they arise even in deep linear networks and depend on depth and data structure. This yields mechanistic insight with cross-disciplinary reach (deep learning theory, neuroscience, transfer learning/negative transfer), and the theory is closely validated by simulations and tested for robustness in nonlinear networks. Paper 1 is a practical, systems-oriented improvement over an existing shuffling method, but is more incremental, has narrower applicability (cloud/object-store training with homogeneous shards), and its impact is weakened by gaps between theory and practice and relatively limited/less reproducible experimental validation. Overall, experts would likely view Paper 2 as more innovative and field-shaping.

Paper 1 introduces a broad, systematic framework for leveraging ML predictions to improve approximation ratios across a wide class of combinatorial optimization problems, with matching lower bounds proving optimality. Its breadth of applicability (Vertex Cover, Steiner Tree, Matching, Knapsack, Max Clique, etc.), practical simplicity, and immediate implementability give it significantly wider impact potential. It opens a new research direction bridging learning-augmented algorithms and approximation theory, likely spawning substantial follow-up work. Paper 2 makes a focused conceptual contribution to Edge of Stability theory—showing EoS occurs under quadratic loss with quadratic parameterization—but is restricted to d=2, single-sample settings with narrow parameter ranges, limiting its scope and generalizability. While technically interesting, its impact is confined to a specialized theoretical niche compared to Paper 1's broad framework applicable across multiple fundamental optimization problems.

Paper 1 is likely to have higher scientific impact because it provides a fundamentally new theoretical understanding of a widely used, search-discovered optimizer (Lion), via a novel Lyapunov analysis that reinterprets Lion as solving a clean constrained/composite optimization problem. This creates a unifying framework (Lion-φ) connecting multiple classic optimization paradigms and opens a broad design space with principled guarantees—impacting optimization theory, algorithm design, and ML practice. Paper 2 targets an important practical pain point (learning-rate robustness) with strong experiments, but its core contribution is a more incremental algorithmic extension of an existing step-size scheme, with the main practical variant lacking theory and the theory focused on convex settings. Thus, Paper 1 offers deeper novelty and broader cross-field theoretical influence while remaining timely for large-model training.

While Paper 1 offers valuable theoretical insights into why multi-task learning works, Paper 2 (Highway-BP) proposes a novel algorithmic modification to backpropagation itself—the fundamental engine of deep learning. By breaking the sequential dependency of backpropagation in residual networks, Paper 2 addresses a critical scalability bottleneck. Its immediate practical application to RNNs (showing 2-3.5x speedups) and its potential to parallelize training for Transformers makes it highly relevant to the current era of massive foundation models. The breadth of architectures covered (ResNet, Transformer, RNN) suggests a wider potential for real-world adoption and engineering optimization than the purely theoretical contribution of Paper 1, which relies on restrictive assumptions (two-layer CNNs, specific activations) unlikely to directly influence practice.

Paper 2 introduces a paradigm shift for DRL-based job shop scheduling by moving from construction to improvement heuristics, with strong empirical results showing large margins over prior DRL methods across classic benchmarks. It offers concrete practical utility (message-passing evaluator, linear scaling to industrial-size problems), proven theoretical properties, and a highly extensible framework likely to catalyze follow-up work. Paper 1, while theoretically interesting in its eigenspace Lipschitz decomposition, suffers from a substantial gap between theory and practice: weak O(log t) convergence rates, bounds requiring expensive-to-compute quantities, small-scale experiments, and no demonstrated practical advantage over existing optimizers. Paper 2's combination of methodological rigor, strong empirical validation, and clear practical applicability gives it significantly higher real-world and academic impact potential.

Paper 2 (CoRe-GD) is likely to have higher scientific impact because it tackles a broad, longstanding scalability barrier in neural graph drawing with a principled hierarchical design and provides stronger methodological rigor (multiple lemmas on expressiveness, optimal scaling, and sub-quadratic complexity) plus extensive empirical validation across diverse graph domains. Its contributions—positional rewiring and replay-buffer training for very deep/recurrent GNN updates—are plausibly transferable beyond visualization to geometric GNNs and positional/structural encoding for graph transformers, increasing cross-field reach. Paper 1 (PABBO) is timely and practically important for human-in-the-loop optimization, but its impact is tempered by pretraining distribution bias, dimension-specific models, and less clear advantages on real preference datasets, making adoption/generalization riskier. Overall, CoRe-GD’s combination of theory, scalability, and breadth makes it more likely to be foundational.

While Paper 1 (SEMA) offers a practical 'free lunch' likely to see adoption in engineering pipelines, Paper 2 addresses a fundamental theoretical gap in constrained learning. Paper 2 provides the first rigorous explanation for why dual ascent methods work in non-convex deep learning without impractical randomization, linking model capacity to constraint satisfaction. This theoretical insight is more novel and scientifically significant than Paper 1's heuristic modification of an existing technique. Paper 2's findings on overparameterization aiding feasibility in constrained settings offer a foundational shift in understanding, whereas Paper 1 is an incremental optimization improvement with weak theoretical grounding.

Paper 2 defines a new class of games (ACCES) with immediate applications in robust combinatorial optimization (e.g., security, logistics), bridging Game Theory and RL. It offers a complete theoretical pipeline (existence proofs, algorithms, convergence analysis) and addresses a practical gap in solving asymmetric problems where one player is combinatorial and the other continuous. While Paper 1 provides a valuable theoretical correction regarding the 'Edge of Stability' in deep learning optimization, its scope is narrower (restricted to specific parameterized regression settings) and its impact is primarily on theoretical understanding rather than enabling new applications. Paper 2's potential to influence how robust optimization problems are modeled and solved across multiple industries gives it broader reach.

Paper 2 addresses a more broadly impactful problem—solving variational inequalities with deep learning—that spans multiple high-profile domains (GANs, reinforcement learning, RLHF, multi-agent systems). Its surrogate loss framework is both theoretically principled (with clean convergence guarantees and a novel separation result) and practically useful (compatible with standard optimizers like ADAM). The unifying perspective connecting existing methods (PHGD, Gauss-Newton) adds conceptual depth. Paper 1 offers a clean preconditioning idea for matrix sensing but addresses a narrower problem, lacks end-to-end recovery experiments, has potentially loose theoretical bounds, and faces scalability concerns. Paper 2's timeliness (given the rise of RLHF and game-theoretic formulations in modern ML), broader applicability, and stronger experimental validation across multiple domains give it significantly higher potential for scientific impact.

Paper 2 offers significantly higher novelty and cross-disciplinary impact. While Paper 1 provides a competent but incremental theoretical analysis of well-known SGD behaviors using standard techniques, Paper 2 establishes a fundamental theoretical insight: that critical learning periods (a phenomenon from neuroscience) emerge even in simple deep linear networks. This finding challenges existing biological assumptions (that specific plasticity mechanisms are required) and connects deep learning theory with neuroscience in a novel way. Paper 2 also provides tractable analytical explanations for transfer learning failures, giving it potential practical relevance in modern pre-training paradigms, whereas Paper 1 largely confirms existing heuristics about batch sizes.

Paper 2 has higher expected scientific impact because it delivers a broadly applicable theoretical unification: a single parameterized HR-ODE framework that subsumes many momentum methods, plus an IQC-based Lyapunov construction that can be reused across algorithms. This creates leverage across optimization, control, and ML theory, and it improves guarantees for multiple established methods (e.g., TM-ODE, QHM) while resolving prior inconsistencies in recovering NAG. In contrast, Paper 1 is timely and practically motivated but is constrained by steep computational cost, limited noisy-scale demonstrations (mostly ≤4 qubits under noise), and impact largely within a niche (NISQ VQA/QAS) where scalability and near-term deployability remain uncertain. Overall, experts would view Paper 2 as more generalizable and field-shaping.

Paper 2 (Switch EMA) has higher expected scientific impact because it is immediately deployable (one-line, zero-overhead change) in the dominant modern deep-learning training pipelines where EMA is already ubiquitous (vision, diffusion, SSL, detection, LM). Its extensive empirical validation across many tasks/architectures/optimizers supports broad, cross-field applicability and rapid adoption, which often drives real-world and community impact even when per-benchmark gains are modest. Paper 1 is more novel and theoretically rigorous, with strong complexity results and near-tight bounds, but its assumptions (strong convexity/compact domain) narrow direct applicability to contemporary nonconvex deep learning, likely confining influence to optimization theory circles. Overall, Paper 2’s practicality, breadth, and timeliness suggest wider uptake and downstream impact.

Paper 2 opens an entirely new research direction by systematically connecting learning-augmented algorithms with approximation theory for offline combinatorial optimization—a gap that had been largely unexplored. Its framework applies broadly to any 'selection problem' (Vertex Cover, Steiner Tree, Matching, Knapsack, Max Clique, etc.), providing a single meta-algorithm with matching lower bounds proving optimality. This breadth and the generative potential for follow-up work across multiple communities (approximation algorithms, learning theory, ML for CO) give it higher impact potential. Paper 1 makes a solid theoretical contribution resolving a theory-practice gap in constrained learning, but its impact is more narrowly focused on the constrained optimization/fairness community. Paper 2's conceptual novelty—using predictions to improve approximation quality at fixed runtime—is more likely to become a foundational framework spawning extensive follow-up research.

Tensor Programs VI makes a deeper theoretical contribution by providing a complete classification of depth-scaling parameterizations and introducing novel concepts (feature diversity exponent, connections to rough path theory) that advance fundamental understanding of neural network scaling. While Paper 1 addresses an interesting engineering challenge of unified CO models, its contributions are more incremental (prefix mechanism, training decomposition) and limited by small problem scales (N=20). Paper 2's Depth-μP prescription, despite being restricted to block depth 1, establishes principled foundations that complement the influential μP width-scaling framework and addresses a critical gap in scaling theory. The theoretical rigor, the completeness of the classification argument, and the potential to become standard practice for depth scaling give it broader and more lasting impact across deep learning research, even though Transformer applicability remains limited.

Paper 1 has higher potential impact due to a clearer novelty leap: it defines a new optimization problem (continual finite-sum minimization) that formalizes sequential/streaming re-optimization for all prefixes, then delivers a substantially improved near-tight first-order complexity Õ(n/ε^{1/3}+1/√ε) plus a complementary lower bound (Ω(n/ε^{1/4}) for a broad class). This combination of new problem framing, algorithmic innovation, and complexity landscape-setting is likely to catalyze follow-on theory and variants beyond strong convexity. Paper 2, while relevant, largely re-derives known SGD upper-bound behaviors under standard smoothness/variance assumptions; its insights (step-decay + small batch) are already common practice and the theory is primarily algebraic manipulation of loose bounds without tightness or new techniques. Hence experts would view Paper 1 as more novel and broadly foundational.

While Paper 1 offers a rigorous theoretical unification of risk management and robust optimization, Paper 2 addresses a critical and timely problem in AI safety: the robustness of watermarking for generative AI. Paper 2's impact is significantly higher because it directly challenges the viability of current technical solutions proposed for AI regulation and provenance. By demonstrating that all major watermarking methods fail against their optimization-based attack framework, Paper 2 sets a new methodological standard for security evaluations in this field. Its findings have immediate implications for policy and industry deployment of AI models, whereas Paper 1 is primarily a conceptual contribution to optimization theory with less direct practical urgency.

Paper 2 has higher potential scientific impact because it provides a rigorous, general theoretical foundation for a widely used, timely optimizer (Lion), using a novel Lyapunov analysis and yielding a unifying Lion-φ framework that connects to broad classes of optimization methods and composite/constrained problems. This advances both theory (new analytical tools, principled interpretation of weight decay as an ℓ∞ constraint) and practice (actionable guidance for tuning λ; interpretability for a memory-efficient optimizer used in large-model training). Its breadth spans optimization theory, deep learning training, and automated algorithm discovery. Paper 1 is a solid systems/data-structure contribution with measurable gains, but its impact is narrower (learned Bloom-filter variants), more engineering-incremental, and constrained by model-class and limited dataset diversity.

Paper 1 (DRL for JSSP) presents a significant paradigm shift in neural combinatorial optimization by successfully applying improvement heuristics rather than construction heuristics to scheduling. Its methodological contributions—specifically the dual-module GNN and the linear-time message-passing evaluator—are rigorous, novel, and have clear utility beyond the specific problem addressed. The paper demonstrates strong empirical performance against state-of-the-art baselines on standard benchmarks. In contrast, Paper 2 (Gradient Descent Stepsize) essentially rediscovers variants of the known Polyak stepsize without adequate citation of prior art or sufficient experimental validation. Its theoretical claims rely on unrealistic assumptions (knowing the optimal objective value) or circular logic for non-convex cases, severely limiting its practical utility and scientific novelty compared to Paper 1.

Paper 2 addresses a more fundamental and broadly applicable question in zeroth-order optimization—identifying the minimum-variance perturbation distribution—yielding a clean, novel theoretical insight (directionally aligned perturbations) that challenges established assumptions in the field. Its conceptual contribution has wider applicability across zeroth-order optimization, LLM fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and black-box optimization, touching a larger research community. The unified framework connecting variance to fourth-order moments provides lasting theoretical value. Paper 1, while technically solid with its constrained three-point lemma, addresses a narrower problem (sparse optimization with specific support-preserving convex constraints) with a multiplicative sub-optimality gap that limits practical significance. Paper 2's timeliness—given the explosion of zeroth-order methods for LLMs—and its potential to reshape perturbation design practices give it higher estimated impact despite both papers having limitations in empirical validation.

While Paper 1 addresses an important biological problem with a clever architecture, it suffers from significant methodological weaknesses (circular evaluation, lack of experimental validation for the design component, unfair baselines). In contrast, Paper 2 provides a fundamental theoretical advance that bridges variational inequalities (VI) and deep learning. By reducing complex VI problems (common in GANs and RL) to surrogate scalar losses solvable with standard optimizers like ADAM, it solves a widespread infrastructure bottleneck. Its theoretical rigor (proving why VIs are harder than minimization via the separation result) combined with practical utility (new, more efficient RL algorithms) gives it broader cross-field applicability and higher potential for lasting impact in machine learning.

Paper 1 (CoRe-GD) is more likely to have higher scientific impact because it delivers a practically scalable, state-of-the-art method for a well-established problem (stress-based graph drawing) while also contributing transferable GNN ideas (hierarchical coarsening/refinement, positional rewiring, latent replay for deep recurrent message passing). Its methodological rigor is supported by clear theory (expressiveness motivation, scale handling, complexity bounds) and broad empirical validation across diverse datasets and baselines, making real-world adoption in visualization and use as structural/positional encodings plausible. Paper 2 offers elegant convex analysis and interpretability, but it substantially departs from standard transformers (removing key-query adaptivity and other core components) and demonstrates limited scalability/benchmark relevance, reducing immediate applicability and breadth of influence.

MEMENTO addresses a more immediate and practical bottleneck in neural combinatorial optimization — efficient inference-time adaptation — with a clean, composable method that achieves state-of-the-art on 11/12 benchmarks. Its practical advantages (no backpropagation at inference, composability with existing solvers, data efficiency, favorable scaling) make it more likely to be adopted and built upon by the active neural CO community. Paper 1 introduces a theoretically novel game class (ACCES) with rigorous existence and convergence proofs, but its practical impact is limited by scalability concerns (only tested up to 50-node problems with hours of training) and relatively weak baselines. While Paper 1 opens a new research direction, Paper 2 delivers more immediate, demonstrable improvements on established benchmarks with broader applicability, stronger baselines, and clearer paths to industrial adoption.

Paper 1 (Highway Backpropagation) introduces a more novel and broadly applicable algorithmic paradigm—parallelizing backpropagation itself by exploiting residual path decompositions—which addresses a fundamental computational bottleneck in deep learning. Its elegant theoretical framework (path decomposition of gradients) provides deep structural insight into residual networks and applies across diverse architecture families (ResNets, Transformers, RNNs). The demonstrated speedups for RNNs are immediately practical, and the approach is timely given renewed interest in recurrent architectures. Paper 2 (NGN-M) is a solid incremental contribution to adaptive optimization, improving learning rate robustness, but operates in a crowded space (Momo, MomSPS, etc.) with more limited novelty. Paper 1's contribution—rethinking how backpropagation itself is computed—has broader potential to influence training paradigms across the field, even if current scale limitations exist.

Paper 2 likely has higher scientific impact because it addresses a central, widely encountered theory–practice gap in constrained ML: why last/best iterates of dual methods often satisfy constraints despite non-convexity, avoiding impractical iterate-randomization. Its conceptual move (parametrized non-convex problems as approximations to convex functional programs) yields general bounds linking feasibility/suboptimality to model richness, directly relevant to fairness, safety, robustness, and RL constraints—domains with strong real-world stakes and cross-field reach. Methodologically, it provides a coherent perturbation-based analysis and extends to stochastic/approximate settings. Paper 1 is elegant and timely for zeroth-order optimization, but its headline practical mechanism (DAP) has a circular dependency on gradient knowledge and its optimality is asymptotic, making real-world dominance less certain and narrowing immediate uptake.

Paper 1 (Approximation algorithms with predictions) offers a higher potential scientific impact. It introduces a foundational, general-purpose framework that bridges the gap between classical approximation algorithms and modern machine learning. Its 'black-box' nature allows it to be applied to a wide range of NP-hard problems (Vertex Cover, Knapsack, etc.) immediately, and the proof of optimality via UGC establishes it as a definitive theoretical result. While Paper 2 addresses an important technical gap in Federated Learning (combining reshuffling and compression), its contribution is more incremental and constrained by significant memory overheads (storing n shift vectors) and convexity assumptions that limit its applicability to deep learning. Paper 1 opens a broader new direction for research in algorithm design.

PABBO makes a substantially more impactful contribution by being the first to amortize Preferential Bayesian Optimization, achieving orders-of-magnitude speedups that enable real-time human-in-the-loop optimization. It introduces a novel transformer neural process architecture trained with RL, sits at the intersection of multiple active research areas (amortized BO, preference learning, RLHF), and demonstrates strong results on both synthetic and real-world benchmarks. The methodology is more rigorous with comprehensive ablations and broader experimental validation. Paper 2, while addressing a valid problem of output-constrained decision trees, feels preliminary with only synthetic datasets, limited scale, no ensemble integration, and relatively straightforward algorithmic modifications. PABBO's timeliness (leveraging the RLHF-driven interest in preference learning) and practical applicability give it significantly higher potential for follow-on research and real-world adoption.

While Paper 1 offers a solid theoretical contribution to optimization with rigorous proofs, Paper 2 provides a more profound conceptual shift. By demonstrating that 'critical learning periods'—a phenomenon deeply studied in neuroscience—emerge from fundamental mathematical properties of deep linear networks rather than biological quirks or nonlinearities, it bridges the gap between deep learning theory and neuroscience. This insight explains *why* pre-training can fail (negative transfer) in a tractable setting, offering broad implications for curriculum learning and foundation models. Paper 1 is technically excellent but addresses a narrower, somewhat artificial optimization niche with restrictive assumptions (strong convexity), limiting its cross-disciplinary reach compared to Paper 2's fundamental insight into the dynamics of learning itself.

Paper 1 (Depth-μP) makes a foundational theoretical contribution by providing a complete classification of depth-scaling parameterizations for neural networks, introducing novel concepts (feature diversity exponent), and establishing rigorous infinite-depth limits via the Tensor Programs framework. While its immediate practical applicability is limited by the block-depth-1 restriction, the theoretical insights are deep and provide principled foundations for scaling deep networks—a critical open problem. Paper 2 (SEMA) is a well-executed practical contribution with impressive empirical breadth, but its novelty is incremental (one-line modification to EMA), its theoretical analysis is shallow, and its gains on large-scale benchmarks are marginal. The scientific community values foundational understanding of scaling principles more highly than modest empirical improvements from a training trick, and Paper 1's framework will likely influence future architectural and optimization research more substantially.

Paper 2 is likely to have higher scientific impact because it targets a broader and rapidly growing class of problems (monotone variational inequalities) that underlie min–max learning, projected Bellman error minimization, and multi-agent/RLHF-style training. Its surrogate-loss framework is both conceptually unifying and directly actionable: it reduces VI solving to sequential scalar minimizations compatible with widely used optimizers (e.g., ADAM), lowering adoption barriers. Theoretical rigor is strong, with explicit convergence rates and a meaningful separation result showing why VI optimization differs from minimization. Real-world applicability is clearer and more immediate via demonstrated gains in RL and min–max settings. Paper 1 is novel and mathematically elegant for SGD near saddles, but is more local/phenomenological and narrower in direct algorithmic leverage, making its near-term cross-field impact somewhat smaller.

Paper 1 represents a significant methodological advance in neural combinatorial optimization (NCO). By successfully demonstrating a DRL-guided *improvement* heuristic (learning to search) rather than the standard construction heuristic for JSSP, it unlocks better performance and scalability, addressing a key bottleneck in the field. The theoretical proof of linear complexity and the novel message-passing mechanism for batch evaluation are robust technical contributions with utility beyond this specific problem. In contrast, Paper 2, while addressing an important biological problem, suffers from weaker methodological rigor (circular validation of the design component using the same model as oracle, unfair baseline comparisons like Pangolin) and lacks experimental validation for its generative claims, severely limiting its immediate scientific impact and reliability compared to Paper 1's solid execution.

Paper 2 has higher likely scientific impact because it provides a rigorous, broadly reusable theoretical foundation for a widely adopted optimizer (Lion), using a genuinely novel Lyapunov analysis and yielding an interpretable constrained/composite optimization view that can guide hyperparameter tuning and inspire new optimizer variants (Lion-φ). This combines strong methodological rigor with immediate relevance to large-scale training where optimizer memory/efficiency matters. Its unifying framework connecting Lion to classical methods broadens cross-field impact (optimization, control/Lyapunov methods, deep learning practice). Paper 1 is innovative and potentially important for parallelizing training, but its practical impact is less certain: it requires architecture-specific engineering, shows limited evidence of wall-clock gains on large Transformers (the dominant setting), and relies on under-explained hacks (e.g., γ) that may hinder adoption. Thus experts would expect Paper 2 to be cited and built upon more widely.

CoRe-GD makes a more substantial contribution by bridging classical graph drawing with modern GNN techniques, introducing novel methodological innovations (positional rewiring, latent replay buffers) with broader applicability beyond its immediate domain. It includes stronger theoretical grounding (WL expressiveness analysis, complexity bounds, closed-form scaling), addresses a genuine scalability bottleneck in neural graph drawing, and demonstrates consistent state-of-the-art across diverse datasets. Its technical contributions (hierarchical coarsening + GNN, positional rewiring) have potential impact across geometric GNN tasks and graph transformer positional encodings. In contrast, Lookbehind is a solid but incremental empirical improvement to SAM, combining existing ideas (multi-step ascent + interpolation) without theoretical guarantees, showing marginal gains at scale (ImageNet), and moving opposite to the efficiency trend in the SAM community.

Paper 1 has higher likely scientific impact due to a broader, more actionable contribution to deep learning scaling: a principled depth-scaling rule (Depth-μP) plus a near-exhaustive classification of depth parameterizations tied to stability, feature learning, and hyperparameter transfer. It offers a simple recipe that could influence training practice for very deep residual networks and extends a widely cited μP framework, increasing adoption potential. Methodologically, it provides rigorous infinite-width/infinite-depth limit results (fully for linear, partially for nonlinear) and introduces reusable concepts (feature diversity exponent) with cross-links to stochastic/rough-path perspectives. Paper 2 is conceptually sharp for Edge-of-Stability theory, but its results are restricted (d=2, narrow step-size bounds, strong conditions) and thus more likely to remain a niche theoretical clarification than a broadly enabling scaling principle.

Paper 2 (Zeroth-Order Optimization) has higher potential impact due to its broader applicability and fundamental theoretical contribution. While Paper 1 is a solid engineering effort within a niche subfield (quantum architecture search), it suffers from severe scalability issues (limited to 4 qubits) and computational costs that limit adoption. Paper 2, conversely, provides a fundamental theoretical insight—identifying a new class of minimum-variance perturbations (DAPs)—that challenges conventional wisdom in optimization. Because zeroth-order methods are currently exploding in popularity for fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) and black-box optimization, the theoretical unification and proposed method in Paper 2 have immediate relevance to a massive, active research community beyond just one domain. The mathematical rigor of characterizing the perturbation distribution offers a lasting contribution to optimization theory.

Paper 1 likely has higher scientific impact due to a more broadly applicable and conceptually novel theoretical bridge: it explains why last/best iterates of dual methods can be nearly feasible in nonconvex constrained learning by relating finite-dimensional parametrizations to convex functional problems. This addresses a prominent theory–practice gap affecting fairness, robustness, and safety constraints across many ML domains, making the contribution timely and cross-cutting. While assumptions and empirical scope are limitations, the core insight—rich parametrization improving feasibility—can influence both theory and practical algorithm design widely. Paper 2 is rigorous and valuable in federated optimization, especially the negative result and DIANA-RR, but its impact is narrower (specific to RR+compression), constrained by convex/unbiased-compressor assumptions and potential memory overhead, which may limit adoption and breadth.

PABBO addresses a clear practical gap—making preferential Bayesian optimization fast enough for real-time human-in-the-loop interaction—with a well-executed solution that achieves orders-of-magnitude speedups. It opens a new research direction (amortized PBO) with immediate applications in interactive design, RLHF-adjacent settings, and personalized optimization. Paper 1, while technically competent in extending convex neural network theory to attention-like mechanisms, analyzes a substantially simplified model that removes the core data-dependent routing of real transformers, limiting its practical relevance. Its experiments are small-scale and the gap between the analyzed model and actual transformers significantly constrains broader impact. PABBO's timeliness (preference learning, amortized BO, human-in-the-loop AI), practical utility, and methodological novelty give it higher expected impact across both the ML methods community and application domains.

Paper 1 (Approximation algorithms) demonstrates significantly higher potential impact due to its broad theoretical framework and rigorous foundations. It opens a new subfield by bridging offline approximation algorithms with learning-augmented algorithms, supported by strong theoretical guarantees (optimality proofs, UGC-based lower bounds) and applicability to a wide class of fundamental NP-hard problems (Vertex Cover, Knapsack, etc.). In contrast, Paper 2 (Output-Constrained Decision Trees) addresses a valid practical problem but suffers from significant limitations: reliance on synthetic data, lack of integration with ensemble methods (where trees are most used), and scalability issues. Paper 1 provides a generative framework that is likely to spawn substantial follow-up theoretical and applied work, whereas Paper 2 is a more incremental modification of existing methods with narrower scope.

Paper 1 introduces a genuinely novel problem formulation (continual finite-sum minimization) with a near-optimal algorithm and matching lower bounds, representing a qualitatively new contribution to optimization theory with clear connections to continual learning. The complexity improvement from O(n²log(1/ε)) to Õ(n/ε^{1/3}) is substantial and the problem formulation itself could spawn a new research direction. Paper 2, while technically sound, is primarily a unification/refinement of existing HR-ODE frameworks with constant-factor improvements in convergence rates for well-studied algorithms in the classical strongly convex setting. Paper 1's novelty in problem definition, significant complexity separations, and timeliness relative to continual learning trends give it higher potential for lasting scientific impact and follow-up work.

Paper 1 offers a substantial theoretical breakthrough by explaining a widely-used, empirically discovered optimizer (Lion). By revealing that Lion implicitly solves a constrained optimization problem, it provides rigorous justification for a popular tool, potentially influencing future optimizer design. The methodology is sophisticated (novel Lyapunov function) and the impact is immediate for the large model training community. In contrast, Paper 2 presents a variant of the known Polyak stepsize with significant practical limitations (requiring knowledge of the optimal objective value) and weaker experimental validation. Paper 1 bridges a critical gap between empirical deep learning and optimization theory, whereas Paper 2 offers an incremental improvement on classical theory with less clear practical utility.

Paper 1 provides the first analytically tractable model explaining why critical learning periods emerge in deep networks, bridging neuroscience and deep learning theory with broad conceptual implications. Its interdisciplinary reach—spanning computational neuroscience, transfer learning theory, and deep learning dynamics—gives it significantly wider impact potential. The elegant demonstration that critical periods arise from depth and data structure alone, without requiring nonlinearities or biological specifics, is a parsimonious and influential insight. Paper 2 makes a solid but narrower contribution to sparse constrained optimization theory with the two-step projection operator and three-point lemma extension, but its impact is largely confined to the optimization theory community. The multiplicative sub-optimality gap and restrictive assumptions further limit its practical reach compared to Paper 1's broad conceptual contribution.

Paper 2 has higher expected scientific impact due to a clearer methodological and empirical advance with broad practical relevance. It introduces a novel DRL-guided improvement paradigm for JSSP (vs. common construction policies), backed by well-motivated GNN architecture design, formal correctness/complexity results (linear scaling; evaluator equivalent to critical path), and extensive benchmark validation including large-scale instances and ablations. Its contributions (learned local search for scheduling + parallel message-passing evaluator) are likely reusable across scheduling and neural combinatorial optimization, enhancing breadth and real-world applicability. Paper 1 raises an important caution about nonsmoothness in DL theory, but much of its theory repackages known subgradient limitations, relies on limited/partially confounded experiments, and offers fewer constructive algorithmic outcomes, reducing novelty and downstream uptake.

Paper 2 has higher impact potential due to a more novel and general contribution: it characterizes minimum-variance perturbation distributions for two-point zeroth-order gradient estimation via a functional-optimization framing and identifies directionally aligned perturbations (DAPs), an overlooked class beyond fixed-length noise. This offers a new design principle (variance linked to 4th moments/kurtosis) with implications across black-box optimization, RL, adversarial settings, and emerging LLM zeroth-order fine-tuning, making it timely and cross-cutting. Methodologically, it provides an elegant variance bound and extends convergence analysis to a broader perturbation family. Paper 1 is largely an algebraic re-parameterization of standard SGD upper bounds under classical assumptions, with limited novelty and weaker practical guidance, so its incremental nature lowers expected scientific impact.

While Paper 1 introduces a novel game-theoretic formulation (ACCES) with solid theoretical backing, Paper 2 presents a more foundational paradigm shift. Paper 2 initiates a systematic study of using machine learning predictions to improve approximation algorithms without increasing runtime. Its framework applies generically to a massive class of 'selection problems' (Vertex Cover, Knapsack, etc.), offering a simple, provably optimal black-box method that bridges the gap between classical approximation algorithms and modern ML-for-optimization approaches. The breadth of its applicability, the elegance of its theoretical bounds (including UGC-based lower bounds), and its immediate relevance to the booming field of learning-augmented algorithms suggest it will be a foundational reference point for future work in theoretical computer science and ML.

Paper 1 (Optimization for Adaptive Attacks) addresses a critical, timely security vulnerability in AI deployments (watermarking for deepfake detection) with a rigorous methodological contribution. By reframing watermark evasion as a formal optimization problem and demonstrating success against all major methods, it fundamentally shifts the burden of proof for future defense research. In contrast, Paper 2 (Local Control Networks) proposes an incremental architectural modification (learnable spline activations) with only marginal empirical gains on simple datasets, lacking the theoretical depth or broad applicability needed to displace existing standards like ReLU-based MLPs or newer innovations like KANs.

Paper 2 is likely to have higher scientific impact because it introduces a new, well-defined game class (ACCES) that formalizes an under-addressed asymmetric combinatorial–continuous setting, and backs it with rigorous theory: Nash equilibrium existence plus convergence guarantees for a tailored double-oracle algorithm, including approximate best-response analysis relevant to RL practice. This creates reusable foundations that can influence multiple communities (game theory, robust/uncertain combinatorial optimization, RL for COPs) and enables principled robustness formulations. Paper 1 is timely and broad, but its core methods (transformer trajectory modeling, prefixing, staged pretraining) are more incremental and its evidence is constrained by small scales and reliance on heterogeneous expert trajectories; impact may be more empirical/engineering and contingent on future scalability. Paper 2’s theoretical contributions are more likely to be built upon.

Paper 1 (Highway Backpropagation) presents a more immediately impactful contribution with a clean theoretical framework that directly translates to practical speedups, particularly for RNNs (2-3.5× demonstrated). Its broad experimental coverage across ResNets, Transformers, and RNNs at ICLR 2025 demonstrates generality. The path decomposition insight into residual networks provides genuine theoretical understanding with practical consequences. Paper 2's eigenspace Lipschitz decomposition is intellectually interesting but suffers from a large theory-practice gap: O(log t) convergence rates are weak, the universal bound requires computing ELMO's expensive steps, Lipschitz estimation is computationally prohibitive at scale, and experiments are small-scale with modest correlations. Paper 1 addresses a timely bottleneck (backprop parallelization) with demonstrated results, while Paper 2's optimizer selection framework remains largely theoretical without practical adoption path.

Paper 1 offers a profound theoretical breakthrough for a widely adopted optimizer (Lion). By proving that an algorithm discovered via evolutionary search implicitly solves a clean constrained optimization problem, it bridges the gap between automated algorithm discovery and rigorous theory. This insight not only demystifies a popular tool used in Large Language Model training but also creates a new generalizable framework (Lion-K) for designing future optimizers. While Paper 2 makes valid technical contributions to heavy-tailed optimization, its scope is more niche (zeroth-order methods, specific noise assumptions). Paper 1's impact is broader because it directly addresses a critical tool in the mainstream deep learning ecosystem, offering immediate practical insights for hyperparameter tuning and explaining empirical behaviors that affect state-of-the-art model training.

Paper 2 presents a rigorous, well-formalized optimization framework for evaluating watermark robustness that carries immediate practical implications for AI governance and industry deployment. Its methodological contribution—surrogate keys enabling gradient-based adaptive attacks—is novel, generalizable, and comprehensively validated across five watermarking methods. The work is exceptionally timely given active policy reliance on watermarking. Paper 1, while presenting an interesting empirical observation about asymmetric momentum, suffers from poor writing, informal theory, extremely narrow experiments (CIFAR-10/100 only), and critically, a 50% improvement rate on CIFAR-100 that essentially amounts to chance. Paper 2's impact parallels Carlini & Wagner's influence on adversarial robustness evaluation, potentially reshaping how watermarking robustness is assessed going forward.

PABBO introduces a genuinely novel paradigm—fully amortized preferential Bayesian optimization—that opens a new research direction at the intersection of amortized inference, preference learning, and black-box optimization. Its orders-of-magnitude speedup over GP-based methods directly enables real-world human-in-the-loop applications that were previously impractical, giving it strong applied impact. The work is also highly timely given the surge of interest in preference-based learning (driven by RLHF) and transformer-based amortized methods. Paper 1, while technically sound and filling a clear theoretical gap (RR + compression in federated learning), is more incremental—combining two known techniques and providing convergence analysis within an already crowded distributed optimization literature. Its practical impact is tempered by memory overhead, convexity restrictions, and limited experimental scale. PABBO's broader cross-field relevance (HCI, design, robotics, RLHF-adjacent) and novelty of the paradigm give it higher potential impact.

Paper 2 (JSSP improvement heuristic) represents a more significant paradigm shift within its field compared to Paper 1. While Paper 1 offers a solid engineering optimization for Learned Bloom Filters (unifying existing approaches), Paper 2 fundamentally changes how Deep Reinforcement Learning is applied to Job Shop Scheduling by moving from construction heuristics to improvement heuristics. This pivot addresses a core representation bottleneck in the field, yielding substantial performance gains over prior DRL methods and scaling to industrial-sized problems. The novelty of the GNN representation and the proven linear scalability give Paper 2 broader potential for high impact in the active field of neural combinatorial optimization.

Paper 2 (Switch EMA) has higher likely scientific impact because it introduces an extremely low-friction training modification (one-line, effectively zero compute/memory overhead) that applies immediately to a vast portion of modern deep learning practice where EMA is already standard (vision transformers, diffusion models, SSL, detectors, language models). Its empirical validation is unusually broad across tasks, modalities, architectures, and optimizers, making adoption straightforward and cross-field impact high. While novelty is incremental and gains can be modest at scale, the “default-practice” potential and timeliness for efficiency-focused training give it outsized real-world influence. Paper 1 is more novel within a specialized niche (noise-aware quantum architecture search) but is constrained by scalability (noisy results mostly ≤4 qubits) and very high training cost, limiting near-term adoption and breadth despite solid methodological contributions.

Paper 1 likely has higher scientific impact because it tackles a central, widely encountered theory–practice gap in constrained ML: dual methods’ lack of feasibility guarantees for last/best iterates in nonconvex settings. Its parametrized-as-convex-functional perspective is a broadly reusable conceptual innovation that can influence analysis and deployment across fairness, robustness, and safety constraints in modern deep learning—highly timely given current regulatory and deployment pressures. While assumptions and computability limits exist, the result directly explains common practitioner behavior and provides general bounds linking model richness to feasibility, with relevance across multiple ML subfields. Paper 2 is technically solid and useful within sparse optimization, but its impact is narrower (restricted constraint class, multiplicative suboptimality trade-offs, and scalability caveats), making it less likely to reshape broader practice.

Paper 1 provides the first analytically tractable model explaining why critical learning periods emerge in deep networks, offering genuine mechanistic insight that bridges neuroscience and deep learning theory. Its clean mathematical framework (exact ODEs matching simulations), elegant recapitulation of classical neuroscience experiments (Hubel-Wiesel), and identification of depth and data structure as key factors represent a parsimonious and testable conceptual advance. Paper 2, while intellectually interesting in reframing SAM through eigenvalue rescaling, suffers from severely limiting quadratic assumptions, insufficient experimental validation (only small-scale benchmarks, no modern architectures), prohibitive computational overhead for marginal gains, and no comparison with recent SAM variants. Paper 1's cross-disciplinary impact spanning neuroscience, learning theory, and transfer learning, combined with its analytical rigor and broader conceptual significance, gives it substantially higher scientific impact potential.

Paper 1 (Type-II Saddles) offers a fundamental theoretical shift in understanding deep learning optimization. By identifying a new mechanism for saddle point entrapment (vanishing noise rather than just curvature) and connecting it to random matrix theory, it addresses a core mystery in the field: why large learning rates at initialization are problematic. This has broad implications for optimization theory and practical learning rate scheduling across all deep learning architectures. Paper 2 (ACCES Games) is a solid contribution to a niche intersection of game theory and combinatorial optimization. While mathematically rigorous, its impact is narrower, limited by the specific asymmetric setting and scalability challenges of the proposed double-oracle RL algorithm. Paper 1 provides a generalizable insight into the dynamics of the most ubiquitous algorithm in AI (SGD), giving it higher potential for widespread scientific impact.

Paper 2 has higher likely scientific impact due to a clearer paradigm shift (DRL-guided improvement vs construction) in an important, applied combinatorial optimization domain, coupled with strong empirical gains on diverse standard benchmarks and demonstrated scalability to very large instances. Its contributions (dual-module GNN for disjunctive graphs, batched message-passing evaluator with proven correctness/linear complexity) are broadly reusable across scheduling and learned local search, giving wider cross-field relevance (RL, GNNs, operations research, industrial optimization). Paper 1 is technically solid and unifies HR-ODE analyses with improved convergence constants, but it mainly advances theory in a mature setting (deterministic strongly convex optimization) with limited practical demonstration; the gains are incremental and narrower in application scope.

Paper 1 (Highway Backpropagation) addresses a more fundamental problem—parallelizing backpropagation itself—with a theoretically elegant framework applicable to the dominant architectures in deep learning (ResNets, Transformers, RNNs). Its path decomposition provides genuine theoretical insight into gradient flow in residual networks, and the demonstrated speedups for RNNs are practically significant, especially given renewed interest in recurrent architectures. The breadth of architectural applicability and the timeliness for scaling deep learning training give it broader potential impact. Paper 2 (Lookbehind) is a solid but incremental empirical improvement to SAM, combining existing ideas (multi-step ascent + interpolation) without theoretical grounding, with marginal gains at scale and a k× computational overhead that runs counter to the field's push for efficiency. Paper 1's contribution is more foundational and has greater potential to influence future research directions.

Paper 1 (CoRe-GD) has higher likely scientific impact due to a more novel synthesis of classical multilevel graph drawing with scalable GNNs, addressing a recognized bottleneck (quadratic costs) with a principled hierarchical design and positional rewiring. It is methodologically stronger, with multiple theoretical results (expressiveness, scale invariance, complexity) and broad experiments against strong classical and neural baselines plus ablations. Its contributions generalize beyond visualization (dynamic rewiring, deep recurrent GNN training via latent replay, scalable structural encodings) and connect to timely needs in graph transformers and scalable geometric learning. Paper 2 tackles an important niche (constraint-feasible multi-target trees) but appears more incremental, with limited synthetic evaluation, unclear scalability, and missing key baselines/ensemble integration—reducing breadth and follow-on potential.

While Paper 1 addresses an important biological problem, Paper 2 (ACCES games) offers a stronger fundamental contribution with broader interdisciplinary potential. Paper 2 defines a novel class of games that bridges game theory, combinatorial optimization, and reinforcement learning, providing rigorous theoretical proofs (NE existence, convergence) alongside a practical algorithm. This framework has wide applicability across operations research, security, and robust optimization. In contrast, Paper 1 suffers from significant methodological weaknesses, specifically the lack of wet-lab validation for its 'design' claims and unfair baseline comparisons (Pangolin). Paper 2's theoretical grounding is more robust, and it opens a new subfield of research for robust RL solvers, whereas Paper 1 is an incremental application of existing architectures with incomplete validation.

Paper 1 (PABBO) has higher expected scientific impact because it introduces a genuinely new, end-to-end amortized framework for preferential Bayesian optimization—extending amortized BO to pairwise preference feedback with a tailored transformer neural process and RL-trained acquisition. This directly removes a major real-world bottleneck (interactive latency), enabling practical human-in-the-loop optimization across design, robotics, and personalization, and is timely given growing interest in preference learning. Methodologically, it is comparatively rigorous with broad benchmarking, multiple baselines, ablations, and clear speed/accuracy gains. Paper 2’s key theoretical stepsize is largely a geometric identity requiring access to the optimum, while the practical method closely matches known Polyak stepsize ideas, omits major related literature, and has limited empirical validation—reducing novelty, breadth, and likely adoption.

Paper 2 (Tensor Programs VI / Depth-μP) addresses a fundamental question about neural network scaling that is central to modern deep learning practice. Its complete classification of depth parameterizations, the novel feature diversity exponent concept, and connections to rough path theory represent deeper intellectual contributions with broader implications. The simple prescriptive rule (scale by 1/√L) has immediate practical applicability for training deep networks with hyperparameter transfer across depths. Despite limitations (block depth 1, heuristic proofs for nonlinear case), the work extends the influential Tensor Programs series and addresses a timely gap as models grow deeper. Paper 1, while technically solid, makes a more incremental contribution to sparse optimization theory with a narrower audience and weaker practical significance due to the multiplicative sub-optimality gap and restrictive assumptions.

PABBO has higher potential scientific impact due to its broader practical applicability and timeliness. It addresses a clear computational bottleneck in human-in-the-loop optimization, achieving orders-of-magnitude speedups that enable real-world deployment scenarios previously impractical. The work opens a new research direction by extending amortized BO to preferential feedback, with clear connections to the booming preference learning ecosystem (RLHF, etc.). While Paper 1 makes a meaningful conceptual clarification about Edge of Stability under quadratic loss, its results are confined to very restrictive settings (d=2, single sample) that limit generalizability. Paper 2's combination of novel architecture design, comprehensive experimental validation across synthetic and real-world benchmarks, and immediate practical utility for interactive preference optimization gives it broader cross-field impact and relevance to a larger research community.

Paper 1 has higher potential scientific impact due to stronger novelty and broader cross-field relevance. It provides what appears to be the first analytically tractable account of critical learning periods in deep networks, isolating depth and data structure as key mechanisms and connecting theory tightly to simulations (and checking persistence in nonlinear nets). This offers conceptual advances for both deep learning theory and neuroscience, with timely implications for transfer/pretraining (including negative transfer). Paper 2, while mathematically clean, largely reframes a greedy optimal stepsize that depends on unknown optima and a practical rule closely related to the well-known Polyak stepsize; its guarantees are narrow and its empirical validation limited, reducing novelty and likely uptake.

Paper 1 (Zeroth-Order Optimization) offers a more fundamental conceptual breakthrough by challenging the long-held assumption that fixed-length perturbations are optimal. By identifying directionally aligned perturbations (DAPs) via a novel fourth-order moment analysis, it opens a new design space for derivative-free optimization. This has high relevance to current trends in memory-efficient LLM fine-tuning (e.g., MeZO), giving it broad potential impact across modern AI. In contrast, Paper 2 provides a solid but more incremental analysis of combining two known techniques (Random Reshuffling + Compression) in Federated Learning. While Paper 2's negative result on naïve combinations is valuable, its proposed solution (DIANA-RR) incurs significant memory overhead (storing n vectors), limiting its practical adoption compared to Paper 1's potential to improve widely used zeroth-order methods.

Paper 1 addresses a fundamental and pressing bottleneck in modern deep learning: the instability of variational inequality (VI) problems found in GANs, multi-agent systems, and reinforcement learning. By reducing VIs to sequences of scalar surrogate losses, it allows the use of standard, powerful optimizers like ADAM, bridging a critical gap between theory and practice. The paper provides rigorous theoretical guarantees (the α-descent condition) alongside concrete, empirically validated algorithms for RL (novel TD(0) variants). In contrast, Paper 2 presents a geometric derivation of the well-known Polyak stepsize without sufficient context or comparison to existing literature (e.g., Loizou et al., 2021). Its practical application is limited by the requirement to know the optimal objective value, and its experiments are far less comprehensive. Paper 1's impact is likely broader and more immediate due to its direct relevance to current challenges in training complex AI systems.

Paper 1 introduces a novel game class (ACCES) and provides a rigorous end-to-end theoretical framework (existence proofs, algorithm design, convergence analysis) for a difficult hybrid problem space. It bridges game theory, combinatorial optimization, and RL in a way that enables new applications in robust optimization. In contrast, Paper 2, while raising valid points about non-differentiability, largely rediscovers known limitations of subgradient methods without offering strong new theoretical mechanisms or constructive algorithmic solutions. Paper 1's constructive contribution to a specific, unsolved problem gives it higher potential for downstream impact in both theory and application.

Paper 1 likely has higher scientific impact due to its stronger novelty and breadth: it provides an analytically tractable explanation for critical learning periods—an observed phenomenon spanning neuroscience and deep learning—showing they can arise even in deep linear networks and identifying depth/data structure as core drivers. Theoretical mechanistic insight with ODE-level analysis, plus validation in nonlinear nets, makes it a foundational contribution that can influence multiple areas (learning theory, transfer learning/negative transfer, and computational neuroscience). Paper 2 is a well-executed systems/algorithm advance within learned Bloom filters, with clear practical wins, but its scope is narrower (boosting-centric, limited datasets) and represents more incremental optimization of an existing hybrid data structure paradigm rather than a broadly general theoretical result.

Paper 1 establishes a broad, systematic framework connecting learning-augmented algorithms with approximation theory — a genuinely new research direction with provable optimality guarantees via UGC-based lower bounds. Its breadth (applying to Vertex Cover, Steiner Tree, Matching, Knapsack, Maximum Clique, etc.) and conceptual clarity give it high generative potential for follow-up work across combinatorial optimization. Paper 2, while theoretically elegant, faces critical practical limitations: no demonstrated wall-clock speedups for Transformers (the dominant architecture), modest experimental scales, and the poorly-understood γ parameter undermining generality. Paper 1's framework is immediately applicable, provably optimal for its problem class, and opens a clear research agenda, whereas Paper 2's impact is more narrowly confined to RNN training acceleration with uncertain scalability to the settings where it would matter most.

Paper 2 has higher likely scientific impact because it provides a rigorous, general theoretical foundation for a widely adopted optimizer (Lion), using a novel Lyapunov construction and yielding a unifying Lion-φ framework that connects to multiple classic optimization methods and composite objectives. This combination of methodological rigor, breadth across optimization/ML theory, and immediate relevance to large-scale training (memory-efficient optimizers) makes it broadly impactful and timely. Paper 1 is conceptually appealing and timely for “foundation models” in combinatorial optimization, but its empirical scope is limited (mostly small N), it depends on heterogeneous expert trajectories, and it does not yet demonstrate scalable, competitive performance against specialist solvers—constraining near-term real-world and cross-field impact.

Paper 2 demystifies Lion, a widely adopted optimizer for large AI model training, revealing it implicitly solves constrained optimization. This insight has immediate practical implications for hyperparameter tuning in large-scale deep learning and provides a unifying Lion-K framework connecting ~10 classical algorithms. The breadth of impact is substantially larger: it affects the massive community training large language models and vision transformers, while Paper 1 addresses a narrower sparse optimization theory audience. Paper 2's novel Lyapunov function construction and the surprising finding that a search-discovered algorithm has clean theoretical structure are more intellectually striking. The timeliness is exceptional given Lion's rapid adoption. While Paper 1 makes solid technical contributions (three-point lemma, TSP operator), its multiplicative sub-optimality gaps and restrictive assumptions limit practical influence compared to Paper 2's direct relevance to state-of-the-art model training.

Paper 2 (Lion Optimizer analysis) has significantly higher potential for broad scientific impact. While Paper 1 addresses a specific theoretical gap in Matrix Sensing, Paper 2 provides a rigorous theoretical foundation for a widely adopted, state-of-the-art deep learning optimizer (Lion). By revealing that an algorithm discovered via evolutionary search implicitly solves a constrained optimization problem, Paper 2 not only validates a popular tool but also establishes a unifying framework (Lion-K) that connects modern heuristics to classical optimization theory. This bridge between automated algorithm discovery and rigorous theory is timely, highly relevant to the massive community training large AI models, and offers a methodology for analyzing other search-discovered algorithms. Paper 1, while technically sound, is narrower in scope and lacks end-to-end validation of its core claim regarding optimization outcomes.

Paper 1 presents a fundamentally more impactful contribution. It formalizes adaptive attacks on image watermarking as an optimization problem, introducing the novel concept of surrogate keys, and demonstrates that all five tested watermarking methods can be broken with negligible quality loss. This has immediate implications for AI governance policies and industry deployments relying on watermarking (e.g., Google's SynthID, EU AI Act compliance). The work establishes a new evaluation standard analogous to Carlini & Wagner's impact on adversarial robustness. Published at ICLR 2024 at a critical policy moment, it challenges fundamental assumptions underpinning regulatory frameworks. Paper 2, while technically sound, offers an incremental improvement to SAM for noisy labels—lacking theoretical grounding, large-scale validation, and comparisons with dedicated noisy-label methods. Its scope and transformative potential are substantially narrower.

Paper 1 has higher likely scientific impact because it targets a core systems-level bottleneck (backpropagation sequentiality) with a broadly applicable algorithmic idea tied to ubiquitous residual architectures, and it is supported by wide empirical evaluation across CNNs, Transformers, and RNNs with demonstrated speedups (notably for RNNs) and a controllable speed–accuracy tradeoff. Even if transformer-scale wall-clock gains remain to be proven, the method aligns closely with real training pipelines and can influence both practice and follow-on research on parallel/approximate gradient methods. Paper 2 is theoretically rigorous but convexifies a substantially altered “transformer” (removing key data-dependent attention behavior and other standard components), with experiments far from real benchmarks and unfavorable scaling, limiting near-term adoption and breadth of impact beyond a niche theory community.

Paper 2 defines a new class of games (ACCES) that bridges combinatorial optimization and continuous game theory, providing rigorous theoretical proofs for existence and convergence which were previously missing. It opens a clear new avenue for research in robust optimization and RL. Paper 1, while proposing a reasonable architecture (LCNs), offers only marginal improvements over existing baselines (MLPs/KANs) on simple tasks and lacks strong theoretical grounding or novelty compared to prior work on learnable activations. Paper 2's foundational contribution to a specific problem class is likely to spawn more follow-up research than Paper 1's incremental architectural tweak.

Paper 1 provides a novel analytical framework explaining why critical learning periods emerge in deep networks, bridging neuroscience and deep learning theory with closed-form solutions. Its interdisciplinary impact spans neuroscience, transfer learning theory, and deep learning foundations. The elegant recapitulation of classical neuroscience experiments (Hubel-Wiesel) in a minimal tractable model is a striking conceptual contribution that appeals to multiple communities. Paper 2 makes a solid theoretical contribution resolving a theory-practice gap in constrained learning, but its impact is more narrowly concentrated in optimization theory for constrained ML. Paper 1's cross-disciplinary reach (neuroscience + ML theory + practical transfer learning insights), its accessibility through analytical tractability, and its potential to reshape how both communities think about developmental learning give it broader and deeper long-term scientific impact.

Paper 1 (ACCES Games) defines a novel game class bridging combinatorial optimization and game theory, providing a complete theoretical framework (NE existence, convergence proofs) alongside a practical RL-based algorithm. This opens a new subfield for robust optimization and applies broadly to operations research and security. Paper 2 addresses a specific theoretical gap in Matrix Sensing (RIP constants) with a clever preconditioning trick. However, its impact is narrower, limited by scalability concerns (O(m²n²) SVD cost), loose theoretical bounds, and a lack of end-to-end recovery experiments to prove that improved RIP constants actually translate to better optimization outcomes in practice.

Paper 1 has higher likely scientific impact because it introduces a genuinely new theoretical classification (Type-I vs Type-II saddles) that overturns a common assumption (strict saddles are escapable by SGD) and connects SGD dynamics to probabilistic stability/Lyapunov exponents via random matrix products—tools with broad relevance beyond a single architecture. Its implications directly explain widely used training practices (e.g., LR warmup, batch-size effects) and offer a transferable conceptual framework for optimization and implicit regularization research. While local/linearized, the methodology is mathematically rigorous and bridges ergodic theory with deep learning in a way likely to inspire follow-on theory. Paper 2 is innovative and application-driven, but its real-world impact is constrained by incomplete demonstrated wall-clock speedups for Transformers, scaling gaps, and reliance on architecture-specific tuning (e.g., the γ hack), making adoption and broad influence less certain.

Paper 2 addresses a highly timely and consequential problem—the security of AI-generated content watermarking—at a critical moment when governments and industry are making policy decisions based on watermark robustness assumptions. Its formalization of adaptive attacks as an optimization problem with surrogate keys provides a reusable evaluation framework analogous to how Carlini & Wagner (2017) set standards for adversarial robustness. The negative results (breaking all five surveyed methods with negligible quality loss in <1 GPU hour) have immediate implications for AI governance, industry deployment (SynthID, etc.), and future watermarking research directions. Paper 1, while a solid engineering contribution to learned Bloom filters with principled DP-based optimization, operates in a narrower subfield with more incremental improvements (24% memory reduction, limited to boosting-based models, two datasets). Paper 2's breadth of impact across security, policy, and multiple research communities substantially exceeds Paper 1's contribution.

Paper 2 addresses a fundamental problem in deep learning (Variational Inequalities) with a rigorous theoretical framework that connects widely used methods (GANs, RL) to standard optimization techniques. Its 'hidden monotone' insight and surrogate loss approach offer a general-purpose solution compatible with existing deep learning infrastructure (like ADAM), promising broad applicability across fields like Reinforcement Learning and Generative AI. In contrast, Paper 1 solves a more niche problem (constrained decision trees) with limited scalability, relying heavily on synthetic data and lacking integration with the ensemble methods that dominate its specific domain.

Paper 2 has higher potential scientific impact due to a more novel and foundational contribution: a principled depth-scaling rule (Depth-μP) plus a near-exhaustive classification of depth parameterizations under clear criteria (stability, nontriviality, faithfulness, feature learning/diversity).
